head: The DUCAR network refers to the District, Urban, and Community Access Roads network in Uganda, representing the largest share of the country’s road infrastructure, accounting for approximately 86.8% (138,510 km) of the total road network. It is managed by local governments with funding from the Uganda Road Fund. Key Aspects of the DUCAR Network Structure: Comprised of district roads (~38,603 km), urban roads (~17,849 km), and community access roads (~79,948 km). Purpose: These roads facilitate economic development by connecting rural communities to markets, schools, and health centers. Maintenance & Funding: Maintenance is handled by local governments, often using the Force Account Policy. The government supports these areas with road machinery and tendered feasibility studies. Challenges: A large percentage of the network is in poor condition, requiring rehabilitation to ensure safety. www.ugandatenders.com www.ugandatenders.com +6 Usage Examples & Context Infrastructure Improvement: The Ministry of Works and Transport uses this framework to distribute machinery to new districts to improve rural accessibility. Funding Utilization: Districts use these funds for localized projects such as upgrading rural roads. Development Projects: The network is central to government efforts to increase production and market access. Condition: More than 40% (up to 60%) is considered to be beyond maintenance level, requiring rehabilitation.

head: Procurement Reference No. UNRA/SRVCS/2021-2022/00072 Section 7. Terms of Reference Terms of Reference for Consultancy services for Road Asset Management & Investment Planning 1.0 INTRODUCTION The Uganda National Roads Authority (UNRA) was established by an Act of Parliament in 2006, and started operations in 2008. According to the Act, the mandate of UNRA is to develop and maintain the national roads network, advise Government on general roads policy and contribute to addressing transport challenges. As of June 2021, UNRA manages a total of 21,010 km of road under its jurisdiction, consisting of 5,398 km paved roads (equivalent to 26%), and 15,612 km unpaved roads (equivalent to 74% of the network). The functional condition of the paved road network stands at 19% in very good condition, 58% in good condition, 16% in fair condition, & 7% in poor condition. For the unpaved road network – 7% is in very good condition, 17% in good condition, 51% in fair condition, 22% in poor condition and 3% in very poor condition. In line with the principles of establishing the Uganda National Roads Authority (UNRA) under the UNRA Act of 2006, the authority is required to give effect to the following: provision of its services in the most economic, efficient and effective manner; management of its affairs in a business-like, cost-effective manner and in accordance with modern management practices and techniques, and in particular, apply best standards of financial management and accounting in i
keyword_paragraphs: Terms of Reference for Consultancy services for Road Asset Management & Investment Planning
1.0 INTRODUCTION
The functional condition of the paved road network stands at 19% in very good condition, 58% in good condition, 16% in fair condition, & 7% in poor condition.
For the unpaved road network – 7% is in very good condition, 17% in good condition, 51% in fair condition, 22% in poor condition and 3% in very poor condition.
In line with the principles of establishing the Uganda National Roads Authority (UNRA) under the UNRA Act of 2006, the authority is required to give effect to the following:

In line with the provisions of UNRA’s Act, the Authority implemented a Road Management System (RMS), supported by a major data collection exercise to populate the RMS with the first batch of data set in 2009-11. A second round of complete network level data was completed in 2017/18 and uploaded in the RMS.

UNRA’s RMS comprises of a Pavement Management System (PMS), Bridge Management System (BMS), Traffic Information System (TIS) and Routine Maintenance Planning and Management System (RMPMS). The comprehensive RMS assists UNRA to generate and justify long-term and short-term investment decisions on all road asset types (roads, bridges, culverts, etc.) – for both capital works (new assets) and preservation and sustainability of the existing assets. The RMS is also used to demonstrate the long-term implications of budget levels and other operational activities on the sustainability of the national road assets. The analysis is undertaken using the dTIMS (Deighton Total Infrastructure Management System) which was customised with the World Bank’s Highway Development and Management (HDM-4) pavement performance prediction algorithms. The optimisation function in (dTIMS) predicts future pavement performance and consequences of alternative funding and policy scenarios. The analysis is able to generate Multi-year preventive maintenance, rehabilitation and upgrading programs for given funding scenarios.  These programs are used as basis for compiling the annual works programs. The routine maintenance interventions are generated separately from the ROMAPS. The RMS and ROMAPS are complimented by the GIS system that links each attribute of a road asset (e.g., link) to its location, hence its role in generation of maps.
There is need to integrate these systems to ensure optimization of the needs analysis and investment options are carried out across different asset types and can fully be reported on maps.

Within UNRA, the RMS is the responsibility  of the Department of Network Planning under the Directorate of Network Planning an Engineering. The RMS is fundamental in ensuring that the mandate of the department under the Directorate of Network Planning and Engineering is met. The Department is mandated to provide technical advice in road asset management in line with industry best practices and take responsibility for upgrading, updating and maintaining the road and bridge Asset Management Systems and recommending appropriate Asset Management Strategies and investment plans.

The department comprises of Head Network Planning, Manager Network Planning, Network Engineer (2 No.), Graduate Network Engineer, and Network Technicians (5 No.) The operations of the RMS are governed by the – i) Road Asset Management Policy; ii) Pavement and Bridge Maintenance Strategies; and iii) Data collection policies and procedures.

In 2016 – 2017 UNRA received technical support for a period of two years with a focus of enhancing the practice of road and Bridge Asset Management. This technical support for RMS was not fully completed due to cancellation of source of funding. Following the cancellation of funding, the Consultant was able to execute up to 65% of the scope of the consultancy services for the duration of the contract under Government of Uganda funding. The key outputs realized include among others:
Pavement and Bridge Maintenance Strategies
Data collection policies and procedures
Migration and validation of the data collected
It is against this background that the UNRA seeks to be supported to be able to realize the full benefit of the scope of services that was envisaged at the time.

Institutionalization of the RMS needs to include a broad range of staff, from top management level through to the technical/operational level. At the top management level, institutionalization needs to bring awareness, buy-in and committed support for RMS, related policies for Road Asset Management, committed resources, and the alignment of the RMS to UNRA's strategic agenda. At the management level, institutionalization also deals with embedding the principles of road asset management at all levels of UNRA. At the technical and operational levels, institutionalization will include enhanced analysis tools, hands on training of technical users of the RMS and building institutional competency to sustain the regular use of the RMS in all asset management planning and decision support processes.

Moreover, in order to retain the confidence and to ensure that the RMS information is reliable, UNRA is to establish a quality control and quality assurance programme that will ensure its road and bridge data is always complete, accurate and up to date. This will entail building institutional competency for data QM planning, data validation and regular updating in the RMS, as an on-going process.

UNRA procured an asset management contract (NERAMP) on one of its strategic corridors – the Tororo-Mbale-Soroti-Lira-Kamdini Road (340km). To ensure sustainability of the initiative and for the road sector to realize the benefits, Institutional support was designed as an integral component of NERAMP. The  component is to provided technical assistance to UNRA  which includes operational support to the UNRA RAMS Division and  this support extends to expanding the scope of the data collection, completing the road network assets inventory, improving information on road condition, improving information on traffic volumes and axle loading, Bridge Management System, road safety, climate vulnerability and extending the analytical capability of the RAMS. Through technical assistance and advisory support, the project will strengthen the processes in UNRA related to network data collection, lifecycle cost analysis, prioritization of investments, multi-year planning and use of RAMS in preparation of annual maintenance plans.
This ToR provides a framework for procuring consultancy services for the technical support for institutionalization and mainstreaming of Road Asset Management System in UNRA.
In light of the above, UNRA now seeks to enhance its RAMS capacity and capability to
Systematically and scientifically assess and identify, select, prioritize and implement road work improvements across its entire road network, and monitor the network performance.

Furthermore, UNRA has also started the journey to become ISO 55000 certified in asset management. ISO 55000 standard series represents a global consensus on what Asset Management (AM) is and what it can do to increase the value generated by agencies. The ISO 55000 series provides an internationally recognized definition of competent Asset Management based on an integrated and continually improving management system. ISO 55001 will help UNRA to create a world class asset management system, build long-term resilience, achieve value for money goals, and meet regulatory requirements. It will help UNRA to replace, upgrade or expand existing assets, determine capital investment, formulate maintenance strategies, and allocate budgets. ISO 55001 helps to add value by providing agencies with an agile framework to help connect asset management with the achievement of corporate strategic goals.

Through this ISO 55000 Initiave, RAMS has attained the much needed visibility and support of Top Management Team (TMT) of UNRA. The ISO 55000 standard also include  integration of other systems to achieve one analysis and planning system across-asset types.
2.0 OBJECTIVES

The main objective of these consultancy services is to provide technical advisory support to UNRA’s RAMS Division to augment its evolving internal capacity and parallelly, to undertake a deep dive diagnostic of the existing platform/systems/tools and procedures.
The specific objectives of the support services are:
Develop institutional competency in data QM planning to ensure data uploaded into RMS is accurate and complete.

Develop institutional competency in the preparation of short, medium and long-term investment planning, including maintenance, rehabilitation and capital works for all asset types (pavements, bridges & structures, etc.). Competency shall also include annual prioritized work plans, performing Life Cycle Cost Analysis (LCCA) and programming routine maintenance on the entire national road network.

Propose and implement a framework for regularly generating a Road Asset Management Plan (RAMP), translating (linking) the RAMP to annual operations (plan implementation), and generating corporate performance progress reporting.
Capacity building and training of competent Asset Managers in UNRA.
Support UNRA in calibration of Data collection systems and implementing automated uploads of data after acceptable quality verification in terms of the QMP.
3.0 SCOPE OF SERVICES

The Consultant shall perform all tasks and services necessary to achieve the objectives outlined above. The anticipated tasks and services necessary to achieve the objectives will include but not limited to those outlined in the following subsections.

TASK S1. The Consultant shall support UNRA to develop individual QMPs with quality verification specifications for all data collected by DCC’s before it is imported into the RMS. The QMPs should ensure that the data imported into the RMS is accurate and complete. The Consultant shall build capacity and competency of the Network Planning Department on developing and maintaining QMPs. The Consultant will also undertake Verification of in-house processes regarding data collection, validation, import to RMS component systems, processing and reporting. This should include post survey processing of ROMDAS roughness and rutting data for each network link. Furthermore, Verification will include visual assessment data from MOBICAP data capture devices and upload into the RMS. The QMP templates shall be updated to include the findings of the verifications, and where necessary, updated and extended to include the above.

TASK S2. The Consultant shall review and enhance the analysis tools in RMS, the principles used, data, environment and the parameters or KPIs used in analysis to align to ISO 55000 Asset Management principles and develop a cross-asset analysis and decision-making tool.

The Consultant will draw from the findings of Asset Management Specialist on required key enhancements of the analysis tools in dTIMS to meet the ISO 55000 Asset Management requirements. This task will include enhancing asset valuation analysis tool for all asset types (pavements, bridges, structures, culverts, etc.) to be aligned to the concept of depreciated asset value (asset value adjusted for current condition).

The analytics should also be capable of fully cross-asset types optimization using asset value and the ‘common currency.’ Further, the analytics should be able to generate projected conditions and asset values (say up 20+ years) for a given budget or resource constraint.

Furthermore, the analytics should be able to output the impact of budget scenarios – i.e. conditions and asset values on color-coded GIS-map of the network. Finally, the analysis tool should include an enhanced flexible dashboard that can summarize or communicate the impact of a given budget allocation by selected KPIs.

The dashboard shall also include the capability to support Top Management to generate summary statistics for dissemination to stakeholders, e.g., linking budget allocation future asset value/conditions, colour-coded maps showing future asset value/conditions, impact of varying resource allocation (what-if scenarios) on key economic indicators, etc.

The Consultant shall therefore upgrade the two dTIMS v9.5 licenses of UNRA to two dTIMS BA (Business Analytics) licenses, configured and established such that all the above-mentioned analytics [i, ii, iii and iv] will be possible.

The Consultant shall carry out any necessary updates of the HDM4 and dTIMS deterioration models and other related analysis variables, e.g., unit costs, etc.  This includes support to UNRA with the establishment of LTPPMSs (Long-term Pavement Performance Management Sections) and the initial annual surveys.
TASK S3. The Consultant shall build the competency of UNRA’s staff on panel inspections – as a mechanism of validating various asset management investment plans generated from RMS.

TASK S4. The Consultant shall undertake capacity building and training of competent Asset Managers in UNRA. The Consultant will carry out a training needs assessment. Design a training plan and programme. Prepare the training materials Conduct on the job training. Define course outline for external trainings. Asses and evaluate the impact of the training. The training shall target all UNRA directorates at the HQ as well as in the Stations, where the enhanced dTIMS analytics will be used for different planning and reporting functions.
Support the Enhancement of Bridge Management System (BMS)

TASK S5. The Consultant shall support the Network Planning Department to develop initial deterioration models for bridges and structures using such concepts as Markov-Chain transition probability models, estimated life spans of bridges and structures, etc. and other lessons from international best practices. The initial models shall make use of all the data available.

TASK S6. The Consultant shall develop or enhance the analysis tool in BMS for investment planning including maintenance, rehabilitation and capital works for all bridge/structural components using optimisation of long-term asset value, trade-off of lifecycle costs and similar parameters including prioritization of bridge due to traffic or access to population. The analysis tool shall be capable of trade-off between different types of bridge components. The Consultant shall provide training and build competence in the Network Planning Department on the use of this enhanced/new analysis tool in dTIMS BA.

TASK S7. The Consultant shall develop an integration of the BMS database and any related GIS map, as well as the new/enhanced analysis tool within dTIMS BA. The primary goal is to achieve a holistic optimisation investment analysis tool in dTIMS BA that can carry out cross-asset investment planning with the objective function of maximizing long-term asset value of all asset types [as detailed under TASK S3, (I, ii, iii and iv)].

TASK S8. The Consultant shall enhance/develop the major culverts module to include an analysis-planning tool with the same features and analysis parameters as for bridges. The module shall be fully integrated into the dTIMS BA and allow cross-asset optimisation for investment planning. [as detailed under TASK S3, (I, ii, iii and iv)].

TASK S9. The Consultant shall support UNRA in developing the guidelines and procedures for conducting analysis (including testing) for safe load carrying capacity of bridges. The Consultant shall support UNRA in applying the guidelines on a number of bridges to illustrate the various approaches.

TASK S10. The Consultant shall undertake capacity building and training of competent Bridge Managers in UNRA. The competence skills shall include developing initial deterioration models with limited historical data, future update of such models with new data, importing the official GIS and RND of a particular year, use of the analysis tool, trade-off between different types of bridge components, optimisation, guidelines and procedures for testing for safe load carrying capacity of bridges, etc.

TASK S11. The Consultant shall review the Traffic Information System (TIS) module with a view of enhancing it to become fully integrated with dTIMS. The TIS that currently is web-based, needs more user-friendly data upload, including data from manual counts and Automatic Traffic Counters (ATC). Integration should address annual import and use of the official common geospatial database (GIS). The TIS needs also to be enhanced in data exchange and information sharing, including workflows for publishing on UNRA website selected data to the public. Furthermore, the dTIMS BA will be enhanced and include a dashboard to generate summary statistics of traffic volumes, axle loads, etc., and colour-coded maps of such parameters – needed for decision-making & stakeholders dissemination.

TASK S12. The Consultant shall undertake capacity building and training of competent Traffic Information Managers in UNRA. The competence skills shall include preparation of QMPs for all data collection, whether manual, by ATC or WIM. It shall include importing the official GIS and RND of a particular year, maintaining and extending the TIS mother and daughter stations relationships, upload procedures, data analysis, data exchange, information sharing, publishing traffic data to UNRA website, etc.
Support Enhancement of the Geographic Information System (GIS)

The separate GIS is the official source of the RND (Road Network Definition) and spatial layout of the UNRA road network, maintained according to the ALRS (Asset Location Referencing System) by the GIS Manager.  Annually, a new version of the GIS is issued to dTIMS, the TIS and BMS, with a log of changes of new roads added or corrections made. The dTIMS, TIS and BMS Managers will then evaluate historic data negatively influenced for either linear location or spatial location, and correct these as necessary during import of the latest official GIS and RND. In any particular year, all RMS component systems should use and report on the same version of the GIS and RND.

The Consultant shall undertake the enhancement of the GIS module in dTIMS to support cross asset analysis of the assets in the PMS and BMS, and where necessary also Right of Way Information System (RoWIS) and the Inventory information from the Inventory surveys.

The integration shall be capable of supporting cross-assets investment analysis and the presentation of outputs on GIS map for all types of assets including pavements, bridges, culverts, right of way facilities, road signs, guardrails, etc. The Consultant shall also update the GIS user Manual.

TASK S14. The Consultant shall undertake training and capacity building of four survey/GIS staff in the Network Planning Directorate to become fully proficient in maintaining the official GIS and RND.
Procure Routine Maintenance Planning and Management System
TASK S15. Develop system requirements specification for RMPMS update / procure / integrate
(i) The Consultant shall customize and deploy the RMPMS in accordance with minimum System Requirements Specifications for Routine Maintenance Planning and Management System below.
View and query road network in GIS interface, including inventory, condition and traffic information
Create budgets for routine maintenance for any office or location
Needs-based budgeting – determine budget requirements by considering road conditions or cyclical budgeting needs.
User-definable maintenance triggers based on conditions or interval-based requirements
Register projects and assign to contractors
Create Bills of Quantities for projects based on maintenance requirements
User-definable lists of activities, including estimated production rates per activity, personnel and materials required per unit of production
Routine maintenance
Control major materials (e.g. cement or premix) and fuel deliveries and issues on a stocktaking basis
Capture a list of maintenance issues for discussion during planning meetings
Public reporting web interface – public can log maintenance issues using web page
Reports on production by activity, team or by road
Capture overhead time such as leave and training time on timesheets
Print reports showing time spending, e.g. idle time, time spent on maintenance, etc.
Shared central database – all users share the same database and data is immediately updated
No software installation is required on users’ computers – use ordinary browsers
16GB RAM
(ii) The Consultant shall prepare user manuals for all levels of access in accordance with the system requirements and functionality.

(iii) The Consultant shall undertake the integration of the Routine Maintenance Planning and Management System (RMPMS) in dTIMS. The RMPMS shall be configured as an enterprise solution sharing common data at the UNRA-HQ while it is primarily used for planning and managing maintenance activities in individual UNRA stations (Districts).

TASK S16. The Consultant shall undertake to upgrade the RMPMS including analysis tools to prioritize all maintenance needs (minor and major rehabilitation), user-friendly workflows to ensure full data interchange between dTIMS and RMPMS, and workflows for consolidating country-wide road maintenance plans (budget requests) from individual stations’ (districts’) plans/budgets. RMPMS shall also be enhanced in automated workflow for capturing periodic (monthly, quarterly and annual) maintenance works progress reports, and a dash-board for communicating selected maintenance related KPIs.

keyword_paragraphs: Introduction

Uganda National Roads Authority (UNRA) was established by an Act of Parliament, the UNRA Act 2006, because of GoU’s road sector reforms aimed at commercialization of road management. UNRA became operational in July 2008 and hence as an organisation, it has been in operation for over 10 years. The Uganda National Roads Authority (UNRA) is responsible for the maintenance and management of the 20,544km National Road Network.
In line with the principles of establishing the Uganda National Roads Authority (UNRA) under the UNRA Act of 2006, the authority is required to give effect to the following:

The Government of Uganda, represented by Uganda National Roads Authority of intends to procure the services of a consultant to carry out Configuration and Calibration of the Highway Development and Management Model (HDM-4) to Uganda conditions aimed at improving decision-making on expenditures in the road sector by enabling effective and sustainable utilisation of the latest HDM-4 knowledge. The configuration and calibration will be done for the latest version of HDM-4 (Version 2.10). HDM-4 simulates future changes to the road system from current existing conditions. The reliability of the results is dependent upon two primary considerations:
How well the data provided to the model represents the reality of current existing conditions and influencing factors, in the terms understood by the model; and
How well the predictions of the model fit the real behaviour and the interactions between various factors for the variety of conditions to which it is applied.

The former relates to the correct interpretation of data input requirements and achieving a quality of input data that is appropriate to the desired reliability of the results. The latter refers to calibration of outputs, and it concerns adjusting the model parameters to enhance how well the forecast and outputs represent the changes and influences over time and under various interventions in Uganda.

The prediction of road deterioration constitutes the backbone for life-cycle analysis and economic assessment in the HDM-4. Thus, the importance of carrying out a proper calibration of the road deterioration models to Uganda conditions can scarcely be overstressed.
It is required that the HDM-4 works activities be adapted to model the performance and effects of the different road works types in Uganda.
Objectives of the Services

The HDM-4 system requires several inputs that require configuration and comprises several models that are designed to be calibrated to local conditions in order to ensure accurate prediction of pavement performance and road user costs. As part of the HDM-4 requirements, it is necessary to configure the system and calibrate the various models.
Asset Value Calculation Parameters
Maintenance and Improvement Standards
Others (Climate zones, speed flow type, traffic flow patterns, road network aggregate data, economic parameters, etc.)
Level 2 Calibration is required for the following models:

Each of these components will be configured and calibrated by the appointed consultant jointly with the aid of staff of UNRA. This section describes the proposed tasks required for calibration and adaptation of HDM- 4 to Uganda conditions.
There are three levels of calibration for the HDM- 4, which involves low, moderate and major levels of effort and resources, as follows:

Level 1 – Basic Application: Determines the value of required basic input parameters, adopts many default values, and calibrates the most sensitive parameters with best estimates, desk studies or minimal field surveys.

Level 2 – Calibration: Requires measurement of additional input parameters and moderate field surveys to calibrate key predictive relationships to local conditions. This may entail slight modification of the model source code.

Level 3 – Adaptation: Undertakes major field surveys and controlled experiments to enhance the existing predictive relationships or to develop new and locally specific relationships for substitution in the source code of the model.

In terms of effort, these levels can be viewed as weeks, months and years. A Level 1 calibration can be conducted in weeks. For a Level 2 calibration there is an increase in the amount of effort required so it will take months. Level 3 calibrations require a long-term commitment to basic data collection so their duration will be for a year or more.

For this project, the target level of the configuration and calibration study is Level 2 calibration, and the calibration will concentrate on the most important parameters as measured by impact sensitivity. The ultimate objective is to adapt the HDM-4 works activities to model the performance and effects of the different road works types in Uganda and also for budgetary planning process.

Impact elasticity is the ratio of the percentage change in a specific result to the percentage change of the input parameter, holding all other parameters constant at a mean value. For example, if a 10 per cent increase in traffic loading causes a 2.9 per cent increase in roughness developed after 15 years, the impact elasticity term of traffic loading for that roughness result is 0.29. If there were a 2.9 per cent decrease, the value would be -0.29.

HDM-4 has four classes of model sensitivity, which have been established as a function of the impact elasticity. The higher the elasticity, the more sensitive the model predictions are. These classes are listed in Table A2.1.
Table A2.1 HDM-4 sensitivity classes Impact

HDM-4 Volume 5 recommends that efforts should be based on the results of these sensitivity analyses. Those data items or model coefficients with moderate to high impacts (S-I and SII) should receive the most attention. The low to negligible impact (S-III and S-IV) items should receive attention only if time or resources permit. One usually assumes the default HDM-4 values for S-III and S-IV items since these will generally give adequate results.

HDM-4 Volume 5 discusses the configuration, calibration and adaptation of HDM-4, thus, shall be used by the consultants as a guide for their work. The consultants shall review the HDM-4 calibration efforts done in other countries.
Scope of  the Consultancy Services.

The Level 2 calibration will be based on analysis of data collected and previous data collected by UNRA as maybe applicable. However, it will be necessary as a must to carry out field surveys as well as additional monitoring of pavement performance and collection of vehicle fleet and traffic data in order to provide complete data sets necessary to achieve this level of calibration.

The Consultant during the calibration of the HDM-4 to Uganda conditions needs to take information from the existing records, historical and present survey of road inventory, pavement condition, traffic, maintenance and improvement standards unit costs of road works, vehicle characteristics, and vehicle operating costs (VOC) such that a Level 2 calibration can be achieved. This will require a careful review and understanding of:
Past working methods and materials used for the construction and maintenance of the road network.
Deterioration profiles for each road surface class and pavement structure type observed from survey data collected.
Current methods of road works, standards and works effects applicable to periodic maintenance; the material properties, expected life and unit costs of works activities.
Subsequently, the Consultant would be required to conduct studies to calibrate the different components of HDM-4 as follows:
Desk study – to identify data and information sources, to collate information and carry out basic analysis

Field surveys and measurements – to collect data on pavement ages, traffic loading, road condition, pavement structural number, pavement type, and drainage environment type; to collect data on vehicle resources e.g. fuel consumption.

Evaluation – to determine the calibration factors and model coefficients, by comparing the initiation and progression of the different distress modes from the collected data and data available from existing records in Uganda against the prediction of HDM-4 (with default parameters).

After the configuration and calibration work is done, the consultants shall setup an HDM-4 workspace containing all the configuration and calibration data collected for the study. This HDM-4 workspace and its accompanying documentation could then be distributed to persons interested on running HDM-4 in UNRA. This HDM-4 workspace shall contain at least:
Vehicle fleet characteristics and calibration parameters
Asset Value Calculation Parameters
Basic maintenance standards
Sample evaluations for programmes module
HDM-4 configuration parameters (traffic flow patterns, speed flow types, accident classes, climatic zones, currencies, aggregate data, road deterioration calibration parameters)

Road deterioration is broadly a function of the original design, material types, construction quality, traffic volume, axle load characteristics, road geometry, environmental conditions, age of pavement, and the maintenance policy pursued. HDM-4 includes relationships for modelling Road Deterioration (RD) and Road Works Effects (WE). The RD relationships are used for the purpose of predicting annual road condition and the WE relationships are used for evaluating road works strategies and estimating road agency resource needs for road preservation and development. Thus, the relationships link standards and costs for road construction and maintenance to road user costs through road user cost models.

The calibration of RD and WE models will ensure that the true deterioration of the pavements are appreciated and that any modification to the HDM-4 source codes can be carried out, to predict the effect of both past works and new works on the future condition of the road infrastructure.

To calibrate the Road Deterioration and Works Effects models (RDWE), will require complete data sets on the different pavement types from different climate zones. For road deterioration, this will include both cross-sectional and time-series data. Pavement data should cover all the surface classes in use in Uganda (bituminous, unsealed and concrete), as well as the different pavement types within each class (e.g. for bituminous pavements: asphalt mix on granular base, surface treatment on granular base, asphalt mix on stabilised base, etc.).

In terms of road works effects, data will be required on the immediate effects of maintenance treatments, unit costs, and unit rates of energy use, for each of the various works activities applicable in Uganda. As some of these data will vary from one region to another (e.g. unit costs), the calibration and adaptation exercise should reflect this.
Adaptation of data – mapping of pavement types, strength measures, condition measures, and roadworks activities to those used by HDM-4.
The calibration should be focused on the roughness deterioration, but should not be limited to roughness, because roughness is the key road condition indicator for an economic evaluation.
The Consultant shall recommend any additional monitoring of pavement performance and collection of data that may be required to refine the calibration of HDM-4 models for Uganda.

Traffic impacts all the other models in HDM-4 and in particular the Road User Effects (RUE) and Road Deterioration and Works Effects (RDWE) models. The results of economic analyses are quite sensitive to traffic data, and most benefits that justify road improvements arise from savings in road user costs. To perform economic analyses in HDM-4, traffic characteristics of roads therefore need to be described and represented at an appropriate level of detail. Traffic volume is a Class I parameter for the RDWE model and annual loading a Class II parameter. Speed and vehicle damage parameters are Class II and III parameters for the RUE model

The calibration of the HDM-4 traffic models will involve collection of data which reflect the different traffic situations (e.g. urban, rural, inter-urban and seasonal), and different road types (e.g. four-lane, two-lane, single-lane). Both motorised transport (MT) and non-motorised transport (NMT) from different regions of Uganda will be considered in the study. Data will also be collected on the accident rates occurring on the various classes of roads and junctions on the UNRA road network.
Prediction of total annual traffic volumes, traffic composition, and growth rate
Vehicle free speeds prediction for the different road surface classes

The calibration of the RUE models will ensure that the predicted magnitude of each VOC component and the relativity between the different components conform to that observed in Uganda. This will require the collection of vehicle fleet data (including non-motorised transport) from different regions of Uganda. The RUE model predicts vehicle operating resources as functions of the characteristics of each type of vehicle and the geometry, surface type and current condition of the road, under both free flow and congested traffic conditions. The operating costs are obtained by multiplying the predicted quantities for the various resource components by the unit costs or prices, which are specified by the user in economic terms.
Representative vehicles - A detailed definition of representative vehicles, both MT and NMT is required.

Vehicle resources - the following components of vehicle resources are considered, and each has its separate model: fuel and lubricating oil consumption, tyre wear, vehicle utilization, parts consumption, maintenance labour hours, capital costs (depreciation and interest), crew hours, and overheads.
Travel time - considered in terms of passenger-hours during working and non-working time, and cargo holding hours.

Vehicle basic characteristics and key calibration parameters (for example: annual utilization, hours driven, service life, number of passengers, power, aerodynamic coefficients, fuel consumption, etc.)

The impact elasticity of RUE parameters is set out in Table 4.2, Chapter 4, Volume 5 of HDM-4 documentation series (Bennett and Paterson, 2000). The most sensitive Class I RUE parameter is the new vehicle price, which affects the depreciation, interest and parts consumption models. The parts consumption model itself is also very sensitive. Priority will, therefore, be given to the calibration of the depreciation and parts consumption models. Most of the Class II parameters affect the depreciation and speed models.
A procedure will be recommended for the regular updating of the vehicle fleet characteristics. This will include the identification of the minimum annual data set required to achieve this purpose.
Other Parameters

There are other general parameters apart from the models that need to be configured to local conditions for the correct application of HDM-4. These include climate zones, road network aggregate data, look-up table for missing data, unit costs of road works, traffic growth sets and economic parameters.

The climate in which a road is situated has a significant impact on the rate at which the road deteriorates and on vehicle movement. Important climatic factors are related to temperature and precipitation. The principal climatic data that is used to model the deterioration of the different categories of roads considered in HDM-4 is described in section 4.1, Part C1 Volume 4 of HDM-4 documentation series (Odoki and Kerali, 2000).

HDM-4 requires the climate parameters to be specified for each distinct climate “zone” within a country. It is currently envisaged that there would be a minimum of three zones: Tropical, Equatorial and Mountainous zones.
Road Network Aggregate Data

HDM-4 requires the calibration of eight Road Network Aggregate Parameter Tables. These contain default data values for use in appraisal when more detailed survey data is not available. These parameters are:

Data is divided into a number of classes such as low, medium or high traffic volumes. For road geometry five classes are used to summarise average geometry from a generally level and straight road to a winding severely undulating road. The number of classes can be varied and should be defined by the consultants, who will also characterize numerically each class.

This consists basically of a list of representative road sections with all the data requirements completed and used as defaults. The missing data for a given road section will assume the default values of a representative section onto which it is mapped.

The consultant will also create a matrix of representative road classes and will estimate the breakdown of the Ugandan road network length considering these road classes. This matrix of road classes could then be used for a network strategic analysis with HDM-4.
Economic Parameters
The economic parameters that need to be defined are:
Reporting/Deliverables

Four weeks after the beginning of the assignment, the Consultant must deliver an inception report, describing fully an understanding of the TOR as well as confirmation of the Consultant's team. The overall methodology, Field surveys to be undertaken, processing techniques, Data collection Plan and Quality Management Plan must be presented at inception for approval and discussion.

The purpose of the report is to provide a basis for a mid-term assessment of the assignment. The report should contain an account of the activity up till now as well as plans for the remaining period of the assignment. A comparison of the results achieved so far with the objectives and milestones expressed in the original plan is of high importance. This report should be prepared and submitted for approval after six months from the start of assignment.

A draft Final Report shall be submitted 9 months after commencement to describe and not limited to the type, quality, quantities and sources of data used, personnel, surveys and data collected, problems encountered and solutions employed, detailed description of the various calibration factors as required by TOR, the results and HDM-4 Configuration.
The consultant key Personnel shall include the following staff and any other as maybe proposed to deliver the assignment:
Team Leader (Highway Engineer/HDM-4 Expert)

The Team Leader shall be a qualified and registered professional engineer with a minimum academic degree in Civil or Highway Engineering, a minimum of 10 years’ proven experience in implementing HDM-4 related services in at least five different countries in Africa. The candidate shall have relevant experience in developing road asset management systems with HDM-4 model with in-depth knowledge of the HDM-4 software, including relevant experience of databases and developing interface between HDM-4 and database.

The transport economist should have a minimum of 10 years experience of economic appraisal in infrastructure projects, proven experience in economic appraisal, and demand forecasting for major road infrastructure investments. Experience in employing economic evaluation tools such as HDM4 is required. A minimum equivalent education comparable to MSc in Economics or Transport and 10 years experience in preparation of Financial and Economic investment studies for Civil works construction projects is desirable.

keyword_paragraphs: Terms of Reference for Consultancy Services to Develop Guidelines for Monitoring Procedures and Equipment for Road Condition Assessment
DUCAR routine maintenance monitoring, condition surveys and equipment guidance
Road network focus: District, Urban and Community Access Roads (DUCAR)
Maintenance focus: Routine maintenance planning, monitoring, budgeting and performance reporting
1.0 INTRODUCTION

The District, Urban and Community Access Roads (DUCAR) network is the largest portion of Uganda's road network and provides the local access links that connect communities to markets, schools, health facilities, public services and the wider national road network. The network is managed through central and local government arrangements and is maintained under constrained annual budgets, with routine maintenance commonly undertaken through force account and contracted works.

A consistent road asset management approach is required so that available maintenance resources are directed to the right roads, at the right time, using transparent evidence. The Client therefore seeks consultancy services to prepare practical terms, procedures, tools and implementation support for strengthening routine maintenance planning and monitoring across DUCAR roads and related bridge assets.

These Terms of Reference are intended to procure a consultant who will produce field-ready outputs, not only a study report. All guidelines, forms, templates, equipment specifications, decision rules and training materials shall be designed for routine use by district, urban and community access road agencies with varying staff capacity and budget levels.
2.0 OBJECTIVES

The main objective of the assignment is to develop practical, standardised and affordable guidelines for monitoring road condition and maintenance performance on DUCAR roads, including the procedures, equipment, forms, data quality controls and reporting arrangements required for routine maintenance decision-making.
The specific objectives are to:
- Define minimum and enhanced road condition monitoring procedures for paved, gravel and earth roads.
- Specify appropriate equipment and survey methods for network-level and project-level condition assessment.
- Develop standard field forms, digital data capture templates and quality assurance procedures.
- Define road condition indicators and intervention triggers for routine maintenance planning.
- Establish reporting formats that translate survey findings into maintenance needs, budget requirements and performance indicators.
- Build the capacity of Client and local government staff to conduct, supervise and use road condition assessments.
3.0 SCOPE OF SERVICES
The Consultant shall perform all tasks necessary to achieve the objectives. The scope shall include, but not be limited to, the following tasks.
- Review existing road inventory, maintenance planning, inspection forms, reporting systems and any road management tools used by the Client and local governments.
- Assess current monitoring practices for paved, gravel and earth roads, including drainage, structures, safety defects and maintenance activity reporting.
- Identify gaps in data completeness, survey frequency, equipment availability, staff capability, quality assurance and use of survey results in routine maintenance programming.
TASK M2. Define road condition indicators and survey levels

- Prepare a practical condition indicator framework covering carriageway, shoulders, side drains, cross drainage, bridges, culverts, road furniture, vegetation, erosion, safety hazards and accessibility constraints.
- Define separate survey levels for rapid routine inspection, periodic network condition assessment and detailed investigation before major intervention.
- Define rating scales that can be applied consistently by local staff, including clear descriptions and photographic examples for very good, good, fair, poor and very poor condition states.
TASK M3. Develop monitoring procedures
- Develop step-by-step field procedures for pre-survey planning, route referencing, chainage control, safety briefing, data capture, photographic records, defect coding, validation and approval.
- Specify survey frequency by road class, surface type, traffic level, rainfall exposure and maintenance risk.
- Prepare procedures for post-rainy-season inspections, emergency inspections and follow-up checks after routine maintenance works.
- Define how community reports and road user complaints can be verified and incorporated into formal monitoring records.

- Prepare equipment specifications for low-cost and enhanced survey methods, including GPS-enabled smartphones or tablets, measuring wheels, straight edges, rut depth gauges, drainage inspection tools, cameras, personal protective equipment and vehicle-mounted survey options where justified.
- Define minimum calibration, maintenance and verification requirements for each equipment type.
- Recommend digital data capture templates capable of collecting inventory, condition, photos, GPS coordinates, intervention needs and estimated quantities.
- Prepare guidance on data storage, version control, backup, user permissions and integration with GIS or road management databases.
- Select representative pilot roads covering paved, gravel and earth roads, different traffic levels and different terrain or drainage conditions.
- Conduct pilot surveys jointly with Client and local government staff to test the procedures, forms, equipment and quality controls.
- Compare ratings between survey teams and refine definitions to improve repeatability and reduce subjectivity.
TASK M6. Develop reporting and performance-monitoring framework
- Prepare standard reporting templates for road condition summaries, maintenance needs, backlog, completed works, network accessibility and routine maintenance performance.

- Define indicators for annual reporting, including percentage of network in maintainable condition, drainage functionality, response time to critical defects, routine maintenance coverage and budget execution.
- Develop map and dashboard requirements for showing condition, risks, planned works and completed interventions.
TASK M7. Training and institutionalisation
- Prepare user manuals, quick-reference field cards, training slides, field exercises and assessment materials.
- Deliver training-of-trainers and practical field demonstrations for Client and local government staff.
- Prepare an implementation plan for rolling out the guidelines, including staffing, equipment procurement, annual survey calendar and quality audit arrangements.
4.0 DELIVERABLES
Deliverable
Understanding of assignment, methodology, detailed work plan, stakeholder engagement plan, data request, pilot selection criteria and quality management plan.
Review of existing monitoring arrangements, systems, forms, equipment, capacity gaps and proposed guideline structure.
Draft Road Condition Monitoring Guidelines
Survey levels, indicators, rating scales, inspection procedures, data capture forms, quality control procedures and reporting templates.
Minimum and enhanced equipment lists, technical specifications, calibration procedures, maintenance requirements and cost estimates.
Pilot Survey and Validation Report
Pilot methodology, field results, repeatability checks, issues encountered, revised procedures and implementation lessons.
Training Materials and Field Manual
Final Guidelines and Implementation Plan
Final consolidated guideline, rollout plan, annual monitoring calendar, staffing and equipment requirements, and monitoring performance framework.
The Consultant shall submit editable Word, Excel, GIS and digital form files in addition to PDF versions of all final deliverables.

The Consultant shall provide suitably qualified personnel with practical experience in road asset management, routine maintenance, data collection, budgeting and institutional capacity building. The following key experts are expected as a minimum; bidders may propose additional staff where justified.
Team Leader / Road Asset Management Specialist
Civil engineering or transport asset management professional with at least 12 years of experience in road maintenance planning, condition assessment and public-sector advisory assignments.
Highway Maintenance Engineer
Experience in routine and periodic maintenance standards, work methods, unit rates, quality control and field supervision.
Road Condition Survey Specialist
Experience with visual surveys, roughness/ride quality, drainage and safety-related condition assessment on paved and unpaved roads.
GIS / Data Management Specialist
Experience designing road referencing, geodatabases, dashboards, survey forms and quality-assured data workflows.
Institutional Development and Training Specialist
Experience preparing manuals, training programmes, change management plans and practical user guidance for government agencies.
Survey Equipment Specialist
Experience specifying, calibrating and supervising road condition survey equipment for low-volume road networks.
- Mobilise qualified personnel, equipment, software and logistics required to complete the assignment within the agreed schedule.
- Consult the Client and relevant road agencies throughout the assignment and incorporate agreed comments into deliverables.
- Transfer knowledge to Client staff through workshops, field demonstrations, manuals and practical coaching.
- Maintain confidentiality of all data and deliver all source files, editable tools and documentation to the Client.
- Provide available road inventory, condition, bridge, traffic, maintenance, budget and expenditure data.
- Facilitate access to selected roads, bridges, local governments, maintenance units and relevant agencies.
- Review and provide comments on deliverables within agreed timelines.
- Provide policy direction on budget categories, maintenance standards and institutional responsibilities.

keyword_paragraphs: Terms of Reference for Consultancy Services to Develop a Rationalised Framework for Road Maintenance Allocation and Prioritisation for the Unpaved Road Network.
INTRODUCTION

A key concern emerging from sector statistics is the overall condition of the unpaved road network. Only about 12.7 % of Uganda's roads are in good condition, while approximately 79.9 % are classified as poor, indicating widespread deterioration and reduced serviceability across the majority of the network. In this context, "road condition" refers to the physical state of a road, including factors such as surface integrity, the presence of defects (e.g., potholes and cracks), drainage performance, and overall ride quality. Poor road conditions increase vehicle operating costs, reduce travel speeds, and compromise safety, thereby undermining the socio-economic benefits that the road network is intended to deliver.

The need for a structured road asset management approach is therefore both urgent and strategic. Road asset management is a systematic process for maintaining, upgrading, and operating road infrastructure, based on performance data, lifecycle costing, and prioritisation principles that maximise value for money. The absence of such a structured approach has often resulted in reactive maintenance practices, inefficient allocation of limited resources, and the diversion of routine maintenance funds to address advanced deterioration that could have been prevented.

In response to these challenges, the Client seeks consultancy services to develop a rationalised, transparent, and operational framework for maintenance planning and prioritisation across the unpaved road network and associated bridge assets.
OBJECTIVES

The overall objective of this assignment is to develop a rationalised, transparent, and repeatable framework for allocating maintenance resources and prioritising interventions across unpaved roads and bridge infrastructure, aligned with national development priorities and sector performance indicators. In this context, rationalisation refers to the application of consistent and objective decision-making criteria across all administrative levels, while transparency implies that verifiable data and clearly defined rules support allocation decisions. Repeatability ensures that the framework can be applied annually using updated data, thereby supporting institutional continuity and long-term planning.
The specific objectives are to:
Develop a prioritisation methodology that converts road and bridge condition information into intervention choices and ranked work programmes.
Define intervention triggers for routine maintenance, spot improvement, drainage improvement, grading, regravelling, resealing, minor rehabilitation, bridge maintenance and bridge repair.
Provide an objective allocation approach across districts, urban authorities, community access roads and bridge assets.
Establish governance arrangements, data requirements, validation procedures and reporting templates for annual use of the framework.
SCOPE OF SERVICES

The scope of services entails developing a comprehensive, operational prioritisation framework that translates available data and budget inputs into actionable maintenance programmes. This framework must address the current disconnect between data availability and decision-making, ensuring that planning processes are evidence-based and aligned with the network's actual needs.

Review the current approach for allocating routine maintenance funds to unpaved roads and bridges, including budget ceilings, expenditure categories, force account practices, contracted works, reporting requirements and approval processes.
Review available inventory, condition, traffic, bridge, drainage, socio-economic, accessibility and maintenance cost data.

Identify weaknesses in current prioritisation, including underfunded routine maintenance, reactive allocation, inconsistent condition data, unclear intervention triggers and limited linkage between budget and expected network outcomes.
TASK P2. Develop classification and data requirements.

Define the minimum data required to prioritise each road or bridge, including road ID, administrative area, road class, surface type, length, traffic or usage proxy, condition score, drainage condition, access function, maintenance history, estimated quantities and unit rates.

Define equivalent bridge data requirements, including structure type, span, condition state, load or safety restriction, approach condition, waterway risk, criticality and required maintenance or repair action.
Develop condition-based triggers that assign appropriate interventions to roads and bridges before ranking, so that each asset is considered for the right type of work.

Differentiate routine maintenance from periodic maintenance, spot improvement, emergency work, rehabilitation and capital upgrading to prevent routine budgets from being consumed by work beyond maintenance level.
Define escalation rules where an asset is beyond routine maintenance and should be referred to rehabilitation or capital works funding.
TASK P4. Design the allocation and prioritisation framework.

Develop scoring criteria and weights for prioritisation, including condition, maintainability, traffic or usage, connectivity to essential services, economic access, climate and drainage vulnerability, safety risk, bridge criticality, equity and readiness for implementation.
Develop rules for balancing preventive maintenance against worst-first repairs, so that the framework protects maintainable roads while transparently identifying backlog.
Define how roads and bridges are compared, grouped or ring-fenced to avoid unsafe bridges being overlooked by road-length-based allocation.
Asset condition/need
Good or fair road condition with functional drainage
Routine maintenance: vegetation control, drainage cleaning, pothole patching, shoulder maintenance, culvert cleaning and minor safety works.
Fair road condition with isolated defects or drainage failures
Prioritise localised defects where low-cost works will protect accessibility and prevent escalation.
Poor road condition, but still maintainable
Very poor road condition or failed pavement beyond maintenance
Do not consume routine maintenance funds except for safety and emergency access; refer to the rehabilitation programme.
Referral to periodic maintenance, rehabilitation or capital improvement funding.
Bridge maintenance, emergency repair, structural assessment or capital bridge works.
Indicative Prioritisation Criteria
Condition and maintainability
Protects access to schools, health centres, markets, administrative centres and production areas.
Reduces repeated failure from water damage.
A higher score for roads where drainage works will reduce rapid deterioration or access disruption.
Ensures structures are considered by risk rather than only by road length.
Minimum service or budget rules across administrative units, subject to data quality and eligibility.
DELIVERABLES

The Consultant shall submit all formulas, source files and editable materials. The framework shall remain usable by the Client without reliance on proprietary systems, unless the Client provides prior written approval.
Deliverable
Methodology, work plan, data request, consultation plan, draft prioritisation principles and pilot selection.
Current Allocation and Data Gap Assessment
Review of planning, budgeting, condition data, intervention categories, unit rates, bridge data and governance gaps.
Draft Prioritisation Framework

The Consultant shall provide a multidisciplinary team of suitably qualified and experienced personnel to deliver the assignment. The team shall collectively demonstrate strong practical and advisory experience in road asset management systems, routine and periodic maintenance planning, network condition assessment, data collection and validation, budgeting and financial modelling, and institutional capacity development within public sector road agencies.

The Consultant is expected to propose a team composition adequate to meet the assignment objectives; however, at a minimum, the following Key Experts shall be included. Additional experts (including non-key staff for field data collection, inspections, calibration, and surveys) may be proposed where justified. All Key Experts shall be fluent in written and spoken English.
Team Leader / Road Asset Management Specialist
Civil engineering or transport asset management professional with at least 12 years of experience in road maintenance planning, condition assessment and public-sector advisory assignments.
Highway Maintenance Engineer
Experience in routine and periodic maintenance standards, work methods, unit rates, quality control and field supervision.
Road Condition Survey Specialist
Experience with visual surveys, roughness/ride quality, drainage and safety-related condition assessment on unpaved roads.
GIS / Data Management Specialist
Experience designing road referencing, geodatabases, dashboards, survey forms and quality-assured data workflows.
Institutional Development and Training Specialist
Experience preparing manuals, training programmes, change management plans and practical user guidance for government agencies.
Experience assessing bridge maintenance needs, risk, criticality and prioritisation for low-volume road networks.
Experience in maintenance budgeting, unit cost analysis, multi-criteria analysis and budget scenario planning.
Team Leader / Road Asset Management Specialist

A Civil Engineer or Transport Asset Management Specialist with at least twelve (12) years of post-qualification experience in road asset management and maintenance planning. The expert shall have demonstrated experience in leading similar assignments, particularly in developing country contexts, and shall be responsible for overall coordination, technical leadership, and delivery of outputs. The Team Leader shall have hands-on experience with Road Management Systems (RMS) and familiarity with international asset management standards such as ISO 55000. The role includes overseeing condition assessment methodologies, maintenance prioritisation, and integration of analytical outputs into decision-making frameworks.
Highway Maintenance Engineer

A Civil Engineer with proven experience in the design, supervision, and quality assurance of routine and periodic maintenance interventions on unpaved road networks. The expert shall be knowledgeable in maintenance standards, work methods, productivity norms, and unit rate development. Experience in field supervision, contractor performance monitoring, and development of maintenance manuals and specifications is essential.
Road Condition Survey Specialist (RCS Expert)

A specialist in road inventory and condition surveys with demonstrated experience in both visual and technology-based data collection. The expert shall be conversant with modern survey equipment and methodologies, including roughness (IRI), distress surveys, drainage assessment, and road safety features. Experience in Quality Management Plans (QMP), equipment calibration, GPS set-up, data transfer, and validation processes is required. The specialist shall also be experienced in linear referencing systems, coordinate transformations, and integration of survey outputs into GIS and RMS platforms.
GIS / Data Management Specialist

A GIS/Geomatics or Data Management professional with experience in developing and managing road network geodatabases. The expert shall be responsible for establishing road referencing systems, ensuring spatial data integrity, and supporting integration between GIS, RMS, and survey datasets. Experience in dashboards, data visualisation, and development of structured, quality-assured data workflows is required. Familiarity with coordinate systems, projections, and data integration techniques is essential.
Institutional Development and Training Specialist

A specialist with experience in organisational capacity building within public sector institutions. The expert shall be responsible for preparing operational manuals, training materials, and structured capacity development programmes. Experience in change management, knowledge transfer, and development of user-friendly guidance tools for non-specialist staff is required. The role includes supporting the adoption of systems such as RMS, PMS, BMS, and TIS within the client institution.
Bridge / Structures Engineer (BMS Specialist)

A Civil Engineer with at least ten (10) years of experience in bridge and structure inspection, inventory development, maintenance planning, and capacity assessment. The expert shall have experience in bridge condition surveys, risk and criticality assessment, and prioritisation of interventions, particularly for low-volume and rural networks. Familiarity with Bridge Management Systems (BMS), data collection protocols, and training of inspection teams is required.

An economist or financial analyst with experience in road maintenance budgeting, lifecycle cost analysis, and economic evaluation of maintenance strategies. The expert shall be responsible for developing unit-cost models, conducting multi-criteria analysis, and preparing budget scenarios for different funding levels. Experience in asset valuation (including depreciated replacement cost concepts) and optimisation of resource allocation across asset classes is required.

An ICT or systems development professional with experience in designing and developing transparent, user-friendly decision-support tools. The expert shall develop Excel-based or equivalent planning tools with clear documentation, embedded formulas, and protection features suitable for non-specialist users. Experience with database systems (e.g., SQL, MS Access), integration with RMS outputs, and dashboard development is essential. Knowledge of system interoperability and automation of data workflows is an added advantage.

The Consultant shall be responsible for mobilising all necessary resources, including personnel, equipment, and software, to deliver the assignment within the agreed timeframe. This includes preparing and implementing structured plans covering work execution, stakeholder engagement, quality management, and risk mitigation. All field activities shall be conducted in compliance with applicable health, safety, environmental, and traffic management standards.
