INTRODUCTIONINTRODUCTION
INTRODUCTION
INTRODUCTION
INTRODUCTION
1.1 Scope of this Document

This document is intended to provide guidelines for the execution of road panel inspections by UNRA in support of the finalisation of the Maintenance and Rehabilitation Plans obtained from a strategic analysis. Such an analysis is normally done to prepare a Road Asset Management Plan (RAMP).

This section provides general background to panel inspections for network level preventive maintenance and rehabilitation (M&R) plans and the panel’s role in confirmation and further scoping of candidate projects in these M&R Plans.

The Road Management System of the Uganda National Roads Authority (UNRA) is a sophisticated asset management system containing inter alia a Pavement Management System (PMS) (including both paved and unpaved roads), a Traffic Information System (TIS) and a Bridge and Major Culvert Management System (BMS).  The PMS system employs the dTIMS decision support and optimisation system as strategic analysis module, allowing the consequences of various policy and budget scenarios on the future health of the road network to be analysed and quantified.  For any scenario a first order optimised multi-year tactical preventative maintenance and rehabilitation plan (M&R plan) for the paved road network is also prepared (similarly, a first order optimised multi-year tactical preventative maintenance and upgrading plan (M&U plan) for the unpaved road network).  The optimisation process considers life cycle costing of various maintenance strategies for every analysis segment in the road network, based on current condition and prediction of future deterioration, future traffic volumes, as well as estimated future funds.  It therefore determines the optimal M&R/M&U plan for each individual segment that was analysed.

As the M&R/M&U plans for the road network are determined based on network level condition data all proposals must be verified through a panel inspection before the scoping (including prioritisation and programming) of the various candidate projects in the M&R/M&U plan can be finalised.  Reasons for this include:
Inclusion of any circumstances not catered for in the PMS analysis;
Prioritisation and programming of recommended treatments.

A panel inspection includes the planning and execution of field visits to road segments with recommended treatments by a group of pavement and resurfacing experts, referred to as a ‘panel’, in order to finalise the details of the proposed projects, where possible.  This inspection may be the final confirmation of actions to be taken for preventive maintenance projects, but rehabilitation projects will require further investigation (eg, material sampling and testing) and scoping before final decisions on actions can be made.

This manual provides guidelines towards the panel inspection as well as the finalisation of the M&R/M&U plans, as far as possible, for the paved and unpaved national road network under jurisdiction of UNRA.

The panel inspection is an indispensable part of the PMS operational cycle.  The results of the panel inspection will be used in the overall planning of the network’s preventive maintenance and rehabilitation program for the next financial year or two and the role that it will play depends on various aspects, including the following:
PMS procedures

Whether consultants are appointed to do initial and detailed evaluations of candidate projects on the M&R/M&U Plans and to prepare proposals on the type of seal or other action to be taken, or whether a unit of UNRA is responsible, the purpose of the panel inspection is then to confirm the priority (urgency) of the candidate projects, based on the visual evaluation of the panel including any external factors known to the panel.  These priorities will then be used to prioritise the candidate projects for further investigation.

The type of action recommended and the related cost may influence the need for further investigation. It may be possible to confirm the need for an application of a diluted emulsion spray or slurry due to aggregate loss through a panel inspection only, since the active distress can be assessed visually by the panel. On the other hand, candidate rehabilitation projects will require further scoping for detailed investigation and design before final programming and execution can take place.
Based on the above-mentioned aspects, panel inspections can have one of two main objectives:
Results used for prioritisation of further investigations only

Here the candidate projects are confirmed and prioritised for further investigation.  Typically, panel inspections of candidate rehabilitation projects and candidate resurfacing projects of important roads will have this objective.
Results used for programming of candidate projects only

Here, the candidate projects are confirmed (eg resurfacing) and the type of seal agreed upon, as well as any pre-treatment, and the urgency for action.  Typically, rejuvenation and some resurfacing projects on less important roads will have this objective.
Other general objectives are:
Superficial confirmation of the network level visual assessment and measured data (but note that this is not the primary purpose of the panel inspection);
Determining of priorities of M&R/M&U projects, in order to confirm the timing of the projects over the first two to three years of the M&R/M&U plans.

Very constrained MTEF budgets can result in candidate projects identified in the Technical Needs analysis scenario not being identified in the MTEF analysis scenario as well.  This can confuse panel members as it may seem that the MTEF scenario missed the identification of these as candidate projects. Head Office PMS analysts on the panel will play a crucial role in clarifying this issue, caused by constrained budgets, to panel members.

It is also sometimes difficult to confirm projects where candidates are identified and programmed for later years (eg years 3 to 5) in the M&R/M&U Plans.  Panel inspections are therefore often limited to assessment of candidate projects for the first 2 years of the M&R/M&U Plans, while cognisance is taken of the recommendations for years 3 to 5.

Figure 1 shows a flow diagram of the activities during a PMS operations cycle as well as parties involved with this process.  From this figure it is clear that the panel inspection is an important link between the network level PMS (planning phase) and the project level (prioritisation and programming execution phase) of the PMS.
Figure1:  Flow diagram for PMS activities

Roughness, Rutting and FWD Measurements are regularly collected through UNRA’s Bump Integrators,   the Laser Rut Bar and the Falling Weight Deflectometer.  The data are stored electronically as International Roughness Index (IRI), mm Rutting and Structural Number.  Data collection is governed by Quality Management Plans (QMPs) to ensure acceptable quality data is available. These data are used in the strategic analyses process in order to determine the tactical level M&R/M&U needs.

Visual Assessments of the paved and unpaved road networks are done according to the standard visual assessment procedures of UNRA.  All the defects on every 1 km road segment are recorded on tablets on the road using the Mobicap data capture software.  Visual assessments are governed by a Quality Management Plan to ensure acceptable quality data.  These defects are used in the strategic analyses process in order to determine the tactical level M&R/M&U needs.
Other Data used in the PMS during the analyses include traffic counts while projects already committed for M&R/M&U actions are also captured.

The Strategic Analysis is done once all data have been imported into the PMS.  The strategic analyses concerns a long-term period to assess the future impact of budget and policy scenarios on UNRA and the road users. From this, the tactical level M&R/M&U plans are prepared for a shorter period, typically of MTF duration or 3 to 5 years.

The processing of the data involves calculation of condition indexes for all segments expressing the current condition, as well as summary reporting of network conditions and extents of occurrence of noticeable defects (termed a ‘situational analysis’). The optimisation process is used mainly for two purposes:  firstly to determine budget requirements in order to satisfy UNRA’s organisational (and asset management) policies and goals and secondly to determine M&R/M&U Plans for agreed budget levels.  It typically also includes a Technical Needs Analysis (ie, unconstrained funding), and at least an analysis of the known MTEF Budget. Factors which are taken into account in the optimisation process include:
Current pavement condition;
Pavement deterioration prediction;
Maintenance strategy with relevant sequential treatment selection;
Current and predicted traffic;
UNRA’s maintenance policies.
4.1 Introduction

A planning meeting/s should preferably be held prior to the panel inspection/s.  The aim with this initial planning meeting is to compile possible field M&R/M&U lists for inspection.  Typically at this stage most of the candidate upgrading projects on the M&U Plans have already been confirmed and even some of the proposed rehabilitation projects on the M&R Plans.  In this case the purpose of the planning meeting would thus be to study the recommended segments with upgrading and rehabilitation recommendations now identified for the first time, and specifically the recommended preventative maintenance actions in terms of the following:

The aim of the meeting would be to compile viable projects for the next two to maximum three years.  This list of projects compiled during the planning meeting will form the basis to work from during the subsequent panel inspection.  It is thus of utmost importance to spend enough time and effort on the compilation of these lists to ensure that the panel inspections will be productive as well as time effective.

Note that, due to the extent of the M&R / M&U Plans, it may be more appropriate to arrange more than one planning meeting and panel inspections.  Individual regions or maintenance sections or combination of adjacent Sections into separate meetings and panel inspections could be considered.
A presentation by the Network Engineers involved in the analysis, to set the scene regarding general network condition and needs (preferably also per Region and its Maintenance Sections).

Visual assessment data, consisting of individual distress ratings, preferably printed from the PMS on look-alike assessment forms, or together with the next bullet in graphical presentation along the length of a road (termed ‘stripmaps’).
Visual assessment condition indices, most recent roughness and rutting measurements, Structural Number, also preferably included in the stripmaps.
Outputs from the PMS optimisation process, especially the M&R / M&U plans;
As-build data and resurfacing information, also preferably included in the stripmaps.
Pavement layer structure data and historical data on application of diluted emulsions and reseals.
Most recent annual pavement related routine maintenance costs.
Historical condition data.
Non-pavement related factors that may influence priority (eg, capacity problems).

Region and Section Heads and their staff (Maintenance Engineers) normally have good experience as well as knowledge of roads within their section areas.  Their inputs to this meeting is therefor of utmost importance.  The Sections must prepare a list beforehand of roads needing urgent preventative maintenance or rehabilitation or upgrading.  Typical problem roads that must be on this list include:
Paved and unpaved roads of which routine maintenance costs are very high.
Network Engineers of Network Planning Unit (as well as staff involved in the surveys for data collection),
Maintenance Engineer/s of the Road Maintenance Unit,
Region and Maintenance Section Heads and/or Engineers involved in road maintenance,
Consultants involved with the PMS strategic analysis (if any), and

If possible, also the consultants who executed the visual evaluations (if any).  (Note that Consultants are normally not involved in the panel inspections based on their data collection appointments, this would be an extension thereof).

On a more general note, to achieve the most benefit from the meeting, and the subsequent panel inspection, the following should be considered for attendance of the planning meeting and the composition of the panel:

For resurfacing recommended projects, person/s with local technical knowledge who can provide input with regard to maintenance history, confirm and assess the urgency of the action recommended and provide input regarding the type of seal required for the current situation.

It is important that all persons attending the planning meeting should fully understand the objectives of the meeting and the resultant panel inspection.  They should also be familiar with the predefined guidelines and procedures. The attendees / panel members should be aware of UNRA’s technical policies and procedures regarding resurfacing, and any problems that have previously been encountered with specific types of reseals, etc.

A chair person shall be appointed for the planning meeting and panel inspection, who shall ensure that the objectives of the inspections are achieved and to facilitate the process of achieving co-operation.

The optimisation process proposes a specific type of action during a specific year for each analysis segment.  It is thus necessary to verify both the action as well as the proposed time of the action during the panel inspections.  It must be realised that the treatment of individual short segments do not always form a practical project size to be put out on contract.  Combination of segments per road or for different adjacent roads may therefore be necessary.  This GIS maps with M&R/M&U proposals highlighted can assist much in achieving this.  This process must be completed during the meeting as far as possible and then verified during the panel inspections.

In general, the meeting should discuss all recommend candidate projects, to achieve the objectives of the panel inspection, ie programming preventive maintenance projects and prioritizing rehabilitation and upgrading projects.
When the number of projects are many and the area to be covered stretched out, it may be more feasible to have separate panel inspections, eg, per region.

The time available for the panel to visit the relevant area, compared to the number of projects to be visited.  It may thus be required to eliminate the projects where there is full agreement between the M&R/M&U Plans and the technical persons attending from the specific area. It must however be remembered that the M&R/M&U Plans do not state recommended treatments (eg specific seal) but only a treatment category (eg, reseal).  Such information shall then be provided by the local technical persons from the area.

Where projects are eliminated from the visit by the panel, the panel inspection forms shall be completed during the meeting. It is then especially important that the recommended action and priority thereof be agreed during the meeting.
With limited budget levels cheaper actions such as resurfacing are favoured rather than rehabilitation, resurfacing is then considered as ‘holding action’.

On lightly trafficked roads resurfacing actions are typically proposed rather than rehabilitation actions, especially with limited funds.  High trafficked roads are normally favoured for rehabilitation actions to the degree that the budgets allow.

Roads with poor riding quality (roughness) will normally not be proposed for resurfacing.  The analysis will ignore these roads until a point in time in future where the condition justifies a rehabilitation action.
A point to remember is that ‘fixing worst first’ strategies do not utilise funds in the best way. The strategic level optimisation rather has a network preventive objective.

Introduction

This manual describes the pavement data collection policies and procedures of UNRA.  It confirms UNRA’s commitment to the use of its Pavement Management System to manage its pavements and gravel roads in terms of its corporate vision, mission and goals, as expressed through its Road Infrastructure Asset Management Policy.
Introduction

Pavement Management System (PMS): A set of tools or methods that assist decision makers in finding optimum strategies for providing, evaluating and maintaining pavements in a serviceable condition over a period of time.
Pavement Management: A management approach used by road authority staff to make cost effective decisions regarding providing, evaluating and maintaining pavements, typically by using a PMS.
Roadway register: The list of all roadways under jurisdiction of UNRA.

Shoulder: When the shoulder is referred to as a surface: The area between the outside edge of the carriageway and the shoulder breakpoint. When shoulder is referred to as a pavement layer: The layer on top of the subbase, or, in the absence of a subbase, on top of the subgrade and lying between the outside edge of the base and the shoulder breakpoint.
Use of the PMS to manage the Network of Paved and Unpaved Roadways

The Directorate of Network Planning and Engineering (Network Planning Unit) of UNRA is responsible for the planning of maintenance and repair interventions for the network of paved and unpaved roads (roadways) under UNRA’s jurisdiction.  For this purpose UNRA has procured and implemented a suitable PMS to assist it in the management of the paved and unpaved roads.  It is therefore a policy of UNRA that this network will be managed with its PMS.  This means that:
All proclaimed roads under the jurisdiction of UNRA shall be captured in the RMS and referenced according to the Location Referencing System of UNRA.
The paved and unpaved roadway register will be established in the PMS and kept up to date to reflect all paved and unpaved roadways under UNRA responsibility.
The required related paved and unpaved roadway information of all roadways will be collected and captured in the PMS and will be kept up to date.
The condition of the paved and unpaved roadways will be inspected according to the inspection frequency policy of UNRA, refer Chapter 6.

The paved and unpaved roadway  registers and condition inspections will at all times comply with the data specifications of UNRA (refer UNRA Data Format Specifications for Road Inventory and Condition Inspections (includes inter alia data formats for visual assessment data, machine-based roughness data, video look-up data)).

The paved and unpaved roadway register compilation and condition inspections will be governed by a Quality Management Plan (QMP) to ensure compliance with the specifications.  The QMP will include, as a minimum, quality control and quality verification and acceptance procedures. These will be in response to, and aimed at ensuring compliance with the roadway data specifications of UNRA.

The needs for maintenance and rehabilitation of the roadways under control of UNRA will be determined annually with the PMS. These needs will be based on the most recent condition inspections, and suitably updated unit costs for treatments as defined in the PMS.
UNRA will use the maintenance and repair needs as determined with the PMS to compile its annual and multi-year work plans for roadway maintenance and repair.

UNRA will budget for the operational expenditure of the PMS consisting of the maintenance of the paved and unpaved roadway register, the maintenance of the condition inspection data according to its inspection frequency policies.  Budgeting will include annual software maintenance fees payable to Deighton Associates Limited for the dTIMS software.

Should in-house staff resources and skills diminish to the point that any aspects of the operation of the PMS are at stake, then UNRA will outsource such functions.  UNRA will ensure that all outsourced functions are governed by the same quality control and verification / acceptance procedures than prescribed in this policy document.

UNRA undertakes to provide PMS staff with all necessary skills to execute all required tasks of the data collection for the PMS.  As required, training will be arranged, whether for data collection or operation of the PMS.  In the same regard, staff will be assisted to obtain access to technical literature, and to attend relevant technical conferences, whether presented locally or internationally.
Collecting and updating road network data
Maintaining a road data bank and Road Management System (RMS)
Collecting road and bridge condition data
Formulating short and long-term road and bridge investment plans
Types of Roadway Data Collection
The types of roadway data to be collected consist of inventory data and inspection data.
Roadway inventory data collection consists of:
Initial inventory data collection (newly constructed roadways):

This shall preferably be done from the as-built drawings.  Arrangements shall be made with the relevant directorate to obtain the as-built drawings for extraction of the required network referencing data. The roadway will then be added to the RMS and PMS for the first time.
Initial inventory data collection (previously constructed roadway, but no as-built drawings available):
In this case, the inventory data shall be obtained in the field during a visit to the roadway.  The roadway will then be added to the RMS and PMS for the first time.
Inventory confirmation:

Ideally, all visits to a roadway will be used to confirm the inventory of the roadway (dimensions, types of components) as previously captured in the RMS and PMS. The regular roadway inspections will be used to confirm the roadway inventory as well. The inventory data of roadways already existing in the RMS and PMS will then be corrected, if necessary.
Roadway inspection data collection consists of:
Routine maintenance inspection:

This is a cursory, visual check carried out by road maintenance personnel who have a good practical knowledge of roadway routine maintenance needs, but who are not necessarily specialists in pavement condition inspection.  These inspections are required for the purpose of planning short-term routine road maintenance.  These inspections will normally not provide data for the PMS and are not considered formal enough for updating the PMS.  They do however provide data for the ROMAPS routine maintenance programme.
Pavement visual condition inspection:

This is a regular inspection carried out by a qualified visual condition inspector under the general supervision of a senior pavement engineer, or alternatively by a senior pavement engineer and forms the backbone of an inspection programme required for the PMS.  It will consist of an examination of all the components of the roadway in order to rate the occurrence of a pre-defined series of defects.  Standardised reporting forms and evaluation criteria will be used to aid in the later computerisation of the inspection results.
Pavement equipment surveys:

Pavement equipment surveys shall be carried out using specialized equipment able to measure the profile of the roadways at high speed, typically for roughness. These surveys are regular surveys carried out by qualified operators using certified equipment under the general supervision of a senior pavement engineer, and forms part of the backbone of the regular inspection programme required for the PMS.

Panel inspections are required to confirm the outputs of the needs determination as obtained through the operation of the PMS and shall be executed by a panel of pavement engineers. The panel members shall include the senior pavement engineer who supervised the visual condition inspections and equipment surveys, and the staff involved in the needs determination that lead to the development of the multi-year preventive maintenance and rehabilitation investment plan.

A special inspection will usually be made in connection with unusual circumstances, such as exceptional loading, with the occurrence of a major weakness or with the reassessment of the roadway against revised specifications and regulations.  They will invariably require the detailed involvement of a specialist pavement engineer.  They are also referred to as ‘emergency’ inspections.
Roadway Data Collection Frequencies
The UNRA policy for roadway data collection frequency is listed below.
Data collection item
Data collection frequency
Initial inventory data collection (newly constructed roadways, not yet on the RMS and PMS)
Initial inventory data collection (previously constructed roadway, not yet on the RMS and PMS)
Once it is discovered that a roadway exists that is not on the RMS and PMS.
Inventory confirmation
As soon as possible when doubt has been raised regarding a roadway’s inventory information.
Routine maintenance inspections
Refer to the ROMAPS data collection policies and procedures
Visual condition inspections
However, once a need has been identified for a special inspection, then this is overridden by the special inspection survey frequencies (see below).
Equipment surveys (only for paved roads)
However, once a need has been identified for a special inspection, then this is overridden by the special inspection survey frequencies (see below).
On conclusion of a draft preventive maintenance and rehabilitation needs determination (ie investment plan).
When required by unusual circumstances, eg due to major weakness noticed during visual condition or other inspections.

Despite the roadway data collection frequency policy as defined above for roadway condition inspections, the scale and frequency of roadway inspections are a matter for engineering judgement and must be decided for each situation.  They depend on many factors including:

The classification or importance of the roadway; the sensible use of limited inspection resources demand that a greater priority be given to the visual inspection and equipment surveys on strategically or economically important routes or corridors than to those routes of lower importance or classification.

The age, condition and history of the roadway; evidently, if deterioration has been observed which requires more frequent monitoring, or if a roadway is suspect for a reason, then closer surveillance of the structure would be justified.
The age of specifically the surfacing is important – there is a window of opportunity to apply preventive maintenance, after which rehabilitation is the only suitable treatment option.
Note that a full inspection is required for network performance monitoring.

Introduction
[Aspects to be considered during the compilation of the QMP are provided in italics between square brackets “[ ]”.  They shall be carefully considered for the specific data collection purpose.

This document is a template for compilation of a Quality Management Plan (QMP) for traffic data collection.  It shall be re-compiled per cycle of data collection or data collection project given the requirements for a QMP under the Traffic Data Collection Policies and Procedures of UNRA.  It confirms UNRA’s commitment to collection of good quality data for use in its Traffic Information System (TIS) to support management of its pavements, gravel roads, bridges and major culverts in terms of its corporate vision, mission and goals, as expressed through its Road Infrastructure Asset Management Policy.

The document is compiled under the premise that a QMP is never finalized, and shall be updated as experience (positive and negative) is gained in executing and validating the data collection.  Such updating may even be required to tighten up certain procedures identified to be issues during a previous or a current cycle of data collection, or to introduce additional checking at various stages.

This QMP is based on NCHRP Synthesis 401, Quality Management of Pavement Condition Data Collection.  It was used as base to introduce a best practice QMP for the Network Planning Unit.  The Synthesis summarized current quality management practice of 55 roads authorities in northern America.   The use of the Synthesis in preparation of the QMP is acknowledged with thanks.

A QMP provides a comprehensive, systematic approach to data collection and processing.  It contains detailed quality verification and acceptance procedures and established guidelines to monitor the entire process].

Quality Control component: The responsibility of the Traffic Engineer and his staff of supervisors, manual counters and equipment operators (whether in-house or out-sourced) and UNRA management (the Network Planning Unit), and

Quality Verification and Acceptance component: The responsibility of UNRA management (via the Quality Auditor or Traffic Data Collection Contract Manager), often delegated to a third party, to ensure independent verification and recommendation for acceptance of the collected data.
[Should the data collection be done in-house, then the data collection team(s) and supervisors will be responsible for compiling both components of the QMP.

Should the data collection be out-sourced, then the data collection service provider(s) will be requested in the tender document to compile a draft quality control component, responding to the data specification.  UNRA (Quality Auditor and Traffic Data Collection Contract manager) will review the submitted Quality Control Component, and finalise this with the service provider prior to the start of the data collection contract. The tender (and contract document) will already contain UNRA’s proposed Quality Verification and Acceptance component.]

‘Quality control includes actions and considerations necessary to assess and adjust production processes to obtain the desired level of quality of pavement condition data’ (refer NCHRP 401).  These activities will then include checks on the equipment used to collect the data, the personnel responsible for the data collection and the data collection process itself.

‘Quality acceptance activities are those that govern the acceptance of the pavement condition data’ (refer NCHRP 401).  This is often referred to as ‘quality assurance’, but the NCHRP does not use the term quality assurance due to the fact that it is more aimed at all the activities focusing on increasing the ability to fulfill requirements for the product or service being produced.  UNRA thus also does not use the quality assurance term in this QMP.
This Quality Management Plan (QMP) governs the data collection, and is aimed at ensuring quality data captured in the TIS.

The over-arching governing principles for traffic data collection are provided in the Traffic Data Collection Policies and Procedures of UNRA. The QMP is a companion document to the Policies and Procedures document, and shall focus on ensuring quality data.  The latter defines the contents of the QMP as follows:
Personnel training and certification
Equipment and method calibration, certification, inspection and verification (if equipment will be used during the data collection)
Data capture verification
Data verification procedures
Software for data checks
Data management
Data management
THIS DATA COLLECTION CONTRACT / CYCLE
[Provide details of the specific data collection contract or in-house cycle of traffic data collection

(when data will be collected, what data will be collected (manual counts, automatic equipment counting, WIM, road types or routes on which data will be collected (paved, unpaved)), name of DCC (Data Collection Consultant), name of data collection contract manager (CM) (consulting engineer supervising the contract) or UNRA quality auditor (if data collected in-house by UNRA)
Where more than one DCC is simultaneously involved in a data collection contract, separate QMPs shall be prepared together with each DCC.
Where UNRA collects data in-house a QMP shall be prepared together with each team.]
DATA COLLECTION EQUIPMENT
[Provide details of the data collection equipment to be used by the DCC (or UNRA for in-house data collection) (select only relevant equipment for this specific QMP from below)]
3.2 Axle load surveys
DATA COLLECTION QUALITY CONTROL PLAN

[IMPORTANT NOTE: It is a specific requirement of all data collection, whether done in-house by UNRA staff or on contract by DCC staff, that the data collection staff and their supervisors significantly contribute to the establishment of the Quality Control component of this QMP.

The successful application of this Quality Control component depends as much on UNRA as employer as on the data collection staff.  The Quality Control component provides a win-win opportunity for both parties:  For UNRA, it means that acceptable data is collected, for the DCC or UNRA’s own data collection staff it means that opportunities are created to ensure that risks in data collection (and thus the need for re-collection) are mitigated as far as possible.  Therefore, the Quality Verification and Acceptance component of the QMP, should not later generate any surprises.

The purpose of the Quality Control Plan (QCP) is to ensure that the people, processes and equipment are all aligned at collecting quality data.  The QCP thus has to do with the actual data collection exercise, detecting problems as soon as possible, before large quantities of data have to be re-collected.
In terms of the Traffic Data Collection Policies and Procedures of UNRA, the contents of a comprehensive QCP typically include the following elements:
Documented (and available) manuals and procedures;
Training, testing and certification of survey personnel;
Equipment and / or process quality verification procedures before starting and during production testing;
Checks for data reasonableness, consistency and completeness; and
Data capture verification and acceptance.
4.2 Personnel training and certification
4.2.1 Objective

Personnel deploying and operating the TIS equipment, shall be properly trained, tested and certified prior to the start of any surveys.  Formal training and testing procedures shall be used.   UNRA and the DCC reiterate that adequate training will improve the consistency and accuracy of the collected data.
4.2.2 Equipment Deployment and Operator Training
Equipment deployment and operator training will consist of theoretical and ‘on-the-job’ training.
All staff ([DCC or UNRA] traffic engineer, other equipment operators) shall be familiar with the available training materials.
The following theoretical training session/s were planned and executed on the dates listed:
On-the-job training was executed on the dates listed:
Training materials: These are all user manuals of the various equipment to be used [list only relevant manuals], ie:
[Automatic Counter User Manual (An electronic copy is available on the RMS, in the TIS user manuals folder, under file name: “……………… .pdf”.
WIM User Manual (An electronic copy is available on the RMS, in the TIS user manuals folder, under file name: “……………… .pdf”.
Copies of these training materials shall be made available to [the DCC (or UNRA staff for in-house data collection)].
Equipment operators shall also be familiar with the equipment calibration requirements and checklists for operational procedures (see 4.3 later) and these shall form part of the training materials.
Training Evaluation
An evaluation system will be introduced to evaluate skills and shortcomings of staff, in order to identify continuing training needs.
Approach to testing, at theoretical training session/s and minimum marks to be achieved:

PAVEMENT MAINTENANCE MANAGEMENT STRATEGY
[This document provides a first draft of a pavement maintenance management strategy of UNRA.
The document is therefore intended to serve as a first draft for UNRA to develop and refine as its own pavement maintenance management strategy.

A comprehensive maintenance management strategy consists of a number of specific components that address the various aspects of maintenance management. The key elements of a maintenance management strategy for UNRA are:

Maintenance management establishment component: A pavement maintenance management strategy will determine the management arrangements for asset maintenance across UNRA. The strategy shall include an outline of the organisational structure that will support management of maintenance at head office, regional, and maintenance station levels, specifically regarding planning, budgeting, procurement and execution of pavement maintenance.  In effect this is already covered in the overall management strategy of UNRA, and it is therefore not addressed in any detail here.

Technical component: In the context of this maintenance management strategy, the focus is on the important aspect of planning of technical activities. The technical component outlines the planning and decision making to be undertaken, including each of the categories of maintenance work that must be incorporated, in an appropriate balance, into the maintenance strategy.  It will be referred to as the “Technical Planning Component”.

Risk assessment component: A risk management strategy identifies how an asset owner intends to manage the risks associated with custodianship of the assets, taking into account potential loss of functionality, perception of stakeholders, loss of reputation, etc.  In the context of this maintenance management strategy, it will focus on the areas of risk exposure, and risk management as integral part of the technical planning and decision making to address explicit uncertainty in the technical planning strategy.   The “Risk Assessment Component” is subject to the overall risk management system and strategy of UNRA.

Budgeting component:  A financial management strategy identifies the asset owner’s approach to financial management. In the context of this maintenance management strategy, the focus is on the important aspect of budgeting for the technical activities.  Budgeting principles, processes and motivations are the focus. It will be referred to as the “Budgeting Component”.  The budgeting component is subject to the overall financial management strategy of UNRA.

Procurement component: A procurement strategy is defined as the skilful planning and managing of the delivery process for pavement maintenance.  It involves a carefully devised plan of action which needs to be implemented.  The procurement component shall be the strategy developed for a portfolio of maintenance projects.   The “Procurement Component” is subject to the overall procurement strategy of UNRA.

Maintenance design / execution component: Maintenance design / execution concerns the design and the physical execution of the pavement maintenance interventions, whether executed in-house or through contractors.   The maintenance design / execution component shall deal with the full portfolio of pavement maintenance projects.

Maintenance review component: The maintenance review component completes the maintenance management cycle of activities through a review process, aimed at reviewing the effectiveness of the maintenance management strategy regarding maintenance management establishment, technical planning, risk assessment, budgeting, procurement and maintenance design / execution.  Adjustments shall then be made as necessary.
UNRA shall adapt and refine this document through continuous use to ensure the strategy deliver the required results.]

INTRODUCTION
This document presents the Pavement Maintenance Management Strategy of the Uganda National Roads Authority (UNRA).
This management strategy concerns the maintenance of the Uganda National Road infrastructure pavement assets, whether paved or unpaved, as entrusted to UNRA.
The Pavement Maintenance Management Strategy is subject to the following:
Data Collection Policies and Procedures, v1.1, March 2016 (specifically the Appendix for pavement data collection)
Quality Management Plan Templates for data collection, v1.0, March 2016  (specifically the Appendix for pavement data collection)
The pavement maintenance management strategy refines and expands on the Strategic Asset Management Plan, with a specific focus on pavements.
“Capability” - measure of the ability of UNRA to achieve its objectives
“Critical asset” - asset having potential to significantly impact on the achievement of UNRA’s objectives
“Investment Plan” – a prioritised strategic (multi-year) program of physical investments for maintenance and development taking into account expenditure ceilings
“Level of service” - parameters or combination of parameters that reflect social, political, environmental and economic outcomes that UNRA delivers
“M&R / M&U Plan” – Maintenance and Rehabilitation Plan (for paved roads) / Maintenance and Upgrading Plan (for unpaved roads)
“Objective” - result to be achieved
“Organization” - person or group of people that has its own functions with responsibilities, authorities and relationships to achieve its objectives, here within UNRA
“Organizational objective” – overarching objective that sets the context and direction for UNRA’s activities
“Periodic maintenance” – longer term cyclical activities eg surfacing rejuvenation
“Plan” - detailed formulation of a programme to achieve an objective
“Preventive maintenance” – overarching term inclusive of periodic maintenance, resurfacing and routine maintenance

“Rehabilitation” – repairs to the pavement in order to restore condition and functionality.  It includes light rehabilitation (overlays and / or reworking of base layer) and heavy rehabilitation (reworking of deeper layers or the addition of a new base layer)
“Resurfacing” – activities initiated by condition, eg regravelling and resealing of the pavement surface using a chip and spray or thin overlay
“Risk” - effect of uncertainty on objectives
“Risk appetite” – the amount and type of risk that an organisation is prepared to pursue, retain or take
“Routine maintenance – Condition” – all condition or event driven repair activities carried out at short notice, eg crack sealing and patching, spot regravelling, reshaping of unpaved roads
“Routine maintenance – Cyclical” – all routine cyclical activities carried out on a daily basis, eg blading of unpaved roads and paved road shoulders
“Special maintenance” – condition initiated activities, eg resurfacing preceded by extensive patching and repair, major damage to roads

“Strategic asset management plan” (SAMP) – documented information that specifies how UNRA’s strategic objectives are to be converted into asset management objectives, the approach for developing asset management plans and the role of the asset management system in supporting achievement of the asset management objectives.  A Strategic Asset Management Plan can also be referred to as an asset management strategy
“Technical manuals” – manuals documenting technical procedures, calculations

“Unit cost” – actual cost of performing a unit of work for a specific activity.  Can vary across regions and will vary whether done in-house or through contract maintenance, therefore a proportional blend of the costs data will be required
“Zero-based budget” – a budget prepared from a ‘zero base’, to achieve strategic goals (e.g. a specific level of service) in which all expenses must be justified
PAVEMENT MAINTENANCE MANAGEMENT STRATEGY CONTENT

This maintenance management strategy deals with the pavement assets of UNRA.  It takes cognisance of the higher level Strategic Asset Management Plan (SAMP), also known as the Asset Management Strategy. The SAMP, again, takes cognisance of the Road Infrastructure Asset Management Policy, the RIAMP.

While the SAMP deals with planning, budgeting, scheduling, design / execution, feedback / reporting and monitoring / evaluation of road infrastructure maintenance, this strategy concentrates on the pavement maintenance management function.

This comprehensive pavement maintenance management strategy consists of a number of specific components that address the various aspects of pavement maintenance management. The key components of the pavement maintenance management strategy are:

Maintenance management establishment component: A maintenance management strategy will determine the management arrangements for asset maintenance across UNRA. The strategy shall include an outline of the organisational structure that will support management of maintenance at head office, regional, and maintenance station levels, specifically regarding planning, budgeting, procurement and execution of pavement maintenance.  In effect this is already covered in the overall management strategy of UNRA, and it is therefore not addressed in any detail here.

Technical component: In the context of this maintenance management strategy, the focus is on the important aspect of planning of technical activities. The technical component outlines the planning and decision making to be undertaken, including each of the categories of maintenance work that must be incorporated, in an appropriate balance, into the maintenance strategy.  It will be referred to as the “Technical Planning Component”.

Risk assessment component: A risk management strategy identifies how an asset owner intends to manage the risks associated with custodianship of the assets, taking into account potential loss of functionality, perception of stakeholders, loss of reputation, etc.  In the context of this maintenance management strategy, it will focus on the areas of risk exposure, and risk management as integral part of the technical planning and decision making to address explicit uncertainty in the technical planning strategy.   The “Risk Assessment Component” is subject to the overall risk management system and strategy of UNRA.

Budgeting component:  A financial management strategy identifies the asset owner’s approach to financial management. In the context of this maintenance management strategy, the focus is on the important aspect of budgeting for the technical activities.  Budgeting principles, processes and motivations are the focus. It will be referred to as the “Budgeting Component”.  The budgeting component is subject to the overall financial management strategy of UNRA.

Procurement component: A procurement strategy is defined as the skilful planning and managing of the delivery process for pavement maintenance.  It involves a carefully devised plan of action which needs to be implemented.  The procurement component shall be the strategy developed for a portfolio of maintenance projects.   The “Procurement Component” is subject to the overall procurement strategy of UNRA.

Maintenance design / execution component: Maintenance design / execution concerns the design and the physical execution of the pavement maintenance interventions, whether executed in-house or through contractors.   The maintenance design / execution component shall deal with the full portfolio of pavement maintenance projects.

Maintenance review component: The maintenance review component completes the maintenance management cycle of activities through a review process, aimed at reviewing the effectiveness of the maintenance management strategy regarding maintenance management establishment, technical planning, risk assessment, budgeting, procurement and maintenance design / execution.  Adjustments are then made as necessary.

The figure shows the components of the maintenance management strategy. They take cognisance of the higher level Strategic Asset Management Plan (SAMP), also known as the Asset Management Strategy. The SAMP, again, takes cognisance of the Road Infrastructure Asset Management Policy, the RIAMP.
Figure 1: Basic presentation of pavement maintenance management strategy

“Asset management plan” – documented information that specifies the activities, resources and timescales required for an individual asset, or a grouping of assets, to achieve UNRA’s asset management objectives

“Predictive action” - action to monitor the condition of an asset and predict the need for preventive action or corrective action

“Strategic asset management plan” (SAMP) – documented information that specifies how UNRA’s strategic objectives are to be converted into asset management objectives, the approach for developing asset management plans and the role of the asset management system in supporting achievement of the asset management objectives.  A Strategic Asset Management Plan can also be referred to as an asset management strategy

In terms of ISO 55000, asset management involves a broad range of principles, concepts and processes that help to translate organizational objectives into decisions and actions on assets to achieve the objectives.  Assets are seen as entities that hold potential or actual value, and asset management enables realization of this value.

ISO 55001 distinguishes requirements that relate to asset management (i.e. the planning and delivery of activities undertaken on assets to derive value) from those that apply to the ‘asset management system’ needed to coordinate and enable asset management activities.  Asset management is the core route to deriving value from assets and this is supported, controlled and sustained by a management system that comprises a number of ‘enablers’ (the organisation, its people, relevant information) and ‘capabilities’ of the system (to do risk management, performance evaluation, continual improvement) (as shown in the top and bottom sections of the figure).
provides a framework for setting our asset management objectives,

This policy is crucial to our continuous development and implementation of a road (and ferry) asset management system to manage the national roads and ferry assets entrusted to us and will ensure and provide a framework for:
achieving our corporate objectives through the optimal and sustainable management of the road and ferry assets entrusted to us,
Political requirements in respect of service delivery, job creation and improved accessibility and mobility.
Road infrastructure asset parameters in respect of data collection and condition and performance levels set for each class of road as documented in relevant policy statements.

Reducing the need for transport generally by integrating land use and transport planning so that homes are close to jobs and the production and demand for goods are located as close together as reasonably possible.

Ensuring that upgrading occurs when necessary and that the total benefits (owner and user benefits) of upgrading significantly outweigh the total cost of capital and maintenance cost of the upgraded facility.

New roads are only built in exceptional circumstances where upgrading and capacity improvements of existing roads are no longer possible, where the density of development is such that additional roads are required or where new routes can result in substantial savings to users.
Operational and Functional - Maintaining roads (whether paved or unpaved) in a good operational and functional condition is essential to minimizing overall transport costs and involves:
Ensuring that the pavement structure is adequate for the traffic loads and is upgraded when required.
Roads are transferred from one authority to another depending on the changing function of the road over time.
We recognize the following stakeholders who will derive benefits and value from our sustained application of our road asset management system:
Road Users – who will benefit from improved road conditions and service levels;
Ferry Users – who will benefit from improved ferry conditions and service levels;
Public Transport Operators – who will reduce operating costs by travelling over good quality roads;

UNRA Staff – who will benefit from being involved in competent and effective road asset management that results in proven value of programmes and policies and enhance the reputation of our organization;
Road (and Ferry) Industry – who will benefit from consistent and regular work arising from programmes and projects;
Taxpayers – who will benefit from reduced taxes due to more efficient and effective use of tax revenues and improved returns on investments;
Citizens – who will benefit from reduced transport costs and the related costs of products;
Funding Agencies – who will value the competent and effective road asset management environment within which their funded projects will be incorporated; and
Ministry of Transport and Works - who will be assured of UNRA achieving its mandated service delivery objectives.
Scope of our Road Asset Management System

As Top Management of UNRA we realise that asset management involves a broad range of principles, concepts and processes that help to translate organizational objectives into decisions and actions on assets to achieve our objectives.  Our road (and ferry) asset management system will be developed to take all of the requirements for asset management into account, also the ‘enablers’ (our organisation, people, information) and ‘capabilities’ (risk management, performance evaluation, and continuous improvement).

Given the external and internal issues referred to and the stakeholder requirements, as well as interfaces with other management systems, as needed, the scope of our information and decision support systems has initially been defined as:
A central road (and ferry) network register, with spatial display in a related Geographic Information System;
A bridge and major culvert management system with bridge and major culvert inventory;
An inventory management system for road furniture, including drainage assets;
A road maintenance management system;
We will document the scope of our road (and ferry) asset management system, including the scope of our information and decision-support systems, appropriately.
The following assets have been defined as within the scope of our asset management:
Land on which the roads are located (where ownership has been transferred)

It is furthermore our intention to develop a road (and ferry) infrastructure asset management strategy (also referred to as a strategic plan for asset management (SAMP – Strategic Asset Management Plan) which will also document the road (and ferry) asset management system to support delivery of our asset management objectives.

That this road infrastructure asset management policy, our strategic asset management plan (strategy) and our asset management objectives to be established are compatible with our strategic direction and objectives;
is considered appropriate to the purpose of our  organization,

establishing (and updating) the strategic asset management plan and asset management objectives;
ensuring that the road (and ferry) asset management system supports delivery of our asset management strategy and asset management objectives;

prevent, or reduce undesired effects; and

As roads authority, we will plan actions to address these risks and opportunities, and taking into account how these can change with time, and how to integrate and implement the actions into our road (and ferry) asset management system processes, and evaluate the effectiveness of these actions.   It will require that we define risk assessment criteria, as well as processes to identify potential asset-related risks, to analyse potential risks (likelihood and consequences); to identify appropriate treatment actions and processes for monitoring and control.

We will consider the requirements of our relevant stakeholders and other financial, technical, legal, regulatory and organizational requirements in our asset management planning process to establish our asset management objectives.
The roadmap for setting our asset management objectives is as follows:

Appendix A – Data Validation and Preparation
Introduction
This RMS User Manual describes how the Road Management System (RMS) software is used to by the Uganda National Roads Authority (UNRA) to help manage the National Road Network.
The RMS as a whole stores and processes road and bridge inventory, condition, traffic and related data for highway planning and programming purposes.

Figure 1 gives an overview of the Road Management System in UNRA. This is taken from the RMS Design Report (July 2009). Not all of those components have been implemented yet. Only the shaded boxes have been implemented to date, and those are covered in this documentation. As additional components are added in future, then this document should be updated to include descriptions.

A RMS Server running Windows 2008 Server runs the dTIMS vs 9.5 database (Uganda_2015). This is implemented on MS SQL Server 2014 (Running Windows Server 2008 R2 Enterprise). This server also holds the back-end of the Bridge Management System (BMS) MS Access database file, and back-end of the Traffic Information System (TIS). It also holds video files and photographs that are referenced by the asset management database.
A GIS Server runs ESRI ArcGIS Server, which operates on MS SQL Server 2014. This is housed on a separate physical server running Windows 2008 Server.
ArcGIS client software is also installed on a PC running Windows
The Bridge Management System (BMS) front-end database also resides on a client PC.
The Traffic Information System (TIS) front-end database also resides on a client PC.
HDM-4 and RED are stand-alone packages that run on a single client PC.
dTIMS Asset Management Database
Introduction

dTIMS vs 9.5 from Deighton Associates Limited (Deighton Total Infrastructure Management System) is a customizable framework application for asset management. dTIMS vs 9.5 server has been configured and implemented in UNRA to manage data on road and bridge assets on the national network; and to perform analysis and reporting of those assets.
Database Structure

This chapter of the manual defines the structure of the dTIMS vs 9.5 database as customised for UNRA. The database consists of many different types of pavement related data e.g. Roads, Road Sections, Inventory Data, and Visual Assessments etc.

Data is held in dTIMS in tables. Several different tables have been established to hold data on the Uganda National Road Network. These tables are summarized in Figure 3, and are described in detail later in this chapter.
Describes extent of road network within administrative boundaries including Districts, Maintenance Stations and Regions
Culvert inventory and condition
Generic table for line feature inventory
Generic table for point feature inventory
Roughness data from ROMDAS
Rutting data from ROMDAS
Video Frame locations
Data Validation and Preparation

Data can be entered into dTIMS by either manually typing data at the user interface, or by importing from external files. Any files to be imported must be of the correct format, and effort must be made to ensure that data is valid prior to entry into dTIMS, otherwise dTIMS may reject it.  Appendix A of this document describes Data Validation and Preparation measures that should be taken prior to loading data into dTIMS.
Information on how to import data can be found in the presentation “Where to start – Situational analysis.pptx”
Introduction

(WARNING:  If you delete the data in this table, you will LOSE all the data in the database. This table can only be changed with Temporal Changes (chapter ‎4.5).   Lengths should only be changed if the Driven Length has changed. (Refer to the Road Network Definition (RND) and Asset Location Referencing System (ALRS) Policies and Procedures)
The Base Table is the base table in dTIMS against which all data is referenced. It is shown in Figure 4.

The RoadName attribute or field is the unique road identifier. Normally this is 4 characters long, with the first character representing the road class. However, for dual carriageway roads, the suffix “N<n>” is added for those road parts that are in the negative direction (the first “N” stands for “negative”; the second <n> is the number of the dual part on the overall road). Roads should be numbered consecutively.

The Name is the unique identifier for the road links. It is composed of 4 characters that are the Road Id (including leading zeroes, if any); “_” and the link name (LINK01). Links are numbered consecutively from the start of the Road. Therefore, in the above screenshot, Link A001_LINK01 is the first portion of Road A001 (from 0 to 17.737 km), Link A001_LINK02 is the second portion of Road A001 (from 17.737 to 41.87 km) etc.

The Length field is the length of the road link in kilometres. These are measured (driven) lengths in terms of the “UNRA Asset Management System - Location Referencing System Policies and Procedures” document.

The Name is the unique identifier for the road section. It is composed of 6 characters. The first 4 characters are the RoadName (including leading zeroes, if any); the last 2 characters are the identifier of the Section within the Road. Sections are numbered consecutively from the start of the Road. Therefore, in the above screenshot, Section A00101 is the first section of Road A001 (from 0 to 2.338 km), Section A00102 is the second section of Road A001 (from 2.338 to 4.308 km) etc.

The Direction field represents the direction of the road section. It is defined as starting at Upper Left (UL), Upper Right (UR), Lower Left (LL) or Lower Right (LR). According to the Uganda Location Referencing System, roads in general have increasing chainage away from Kampala. Therefore, roads starting in Kampala and going eastwards will have a direction of LL, roads starting in Kampala and going westwards will have a direction of LR etc. This field is used to help build the road network layer in the GIS (see Chapter ‎6).
The video_file_name attribute represents the name of the video file that contains video data on the section. See ‎4.14 on Page 27 for information on viewing video and/or video frames.

Location Referencing Points (LRPs) are stored in the Point Features table. The Point Features table is used to store point inventory features including Km Posts, Road Signs, Junctions etc. Figure 7 shows Kilometre Posts in the table. These can be filtered by enabling the filter on the feature_type column and selecting “KMP” from the drop-down box.
The Feature Type for kilometre posts is KMP. Note that other point features may be stored in this table (see Page 21).
The Inspector is the name given to the survey team that collected the field information on this feature.
The survey_date is the date on which information on this feature was collected.

dTIMS vs 9.5 has a Temporal LRM functionality; this allows the network to be rolled back to previous points in time, without changing the source data or causing data integrity or referential problems.

The temporal LRM functionality gives users more control over the time dimension of the base elements.  Each Base element has a date range describing the period of time in which that element is considered valid.  This data range is then compared to the current date to decide which road should be displayed at present.
Pick the action required from the dropdown box.
Administrative Data

A series of “administrative” tables has been established to allow the user to define the extent of road sections within Districts, Maintenance Stations and Regions. These data are normally derived from electronic mapping, or from physical markers in the field identifying administrative boundaries. They should change infrequently.
the chainage of the start of the road section that lies within the Maintenance Station
Station is the name of the Maintenance Station.
Culverts are stored in the Culverts table as shown in Figure 14. The Culverts table is a point table designed purely to hold minor culvert data.  Major Culvert data is stored in the BMS.

Most of the culvert attributes represent inventory data (i.e. the type and size of the culvert), and these are normally picked up as part of a regular road inventory survey or inventory update procedure.

Culvert Condition is the condition of the culvert. Permitted values are “G” (Good: < 10% blockage), “F” (Fair: 10 – 50% blockage), “P” (Poor: 50 – 90% blockage) or “S” (Silted: > 90% blockage).  (Not a required field, can be empty)
The Inspector is the name given to the survey team that collected the field information on this feature.
The Survey Date is the date on which information on this feature was collected.

The Line Features table (see Figure 15) is a generic table designed to hold linear inventory features. These are data that can be described and referenced by a start chainage and end chainage along a road section. They include items such as Medians, Pavement Markings, and Side Slopes. These are normally picked up as part of a regular road inventory survey or inventory update procedure.

Feature Type defines the type of line feature stored. Current permitted values are: DRNG (Drainage), FOOT (Footways), LANES (Number of Lanes), MED (Median), RRST (Roadside Structures), TERR (Terrain), and SSLP (Side Slope).
Lined Covered Ditch
The Comment field holds an optional free-text comment recorded by the surveyor for any feature.
The Inspector is the name given to the survey team that collected the field information on this feature.
The Survey Date is the date on which information on this feature was collected.

The Pavement Strength table (see Figure 16) is designed to hold information on pavement strength and history. Strength data are normally derived from analysis of various types of survey including FWD (Falling Weight Deflectometer) and DCP (Dynamic Cone Penetrometer) surveys. Pavement history data are normally initialised from analysis of historical construction records or as-built drawings. However, going forward, UNRA should ensure that these data are updated upon completion of road rehabilitation or construction works.
These data are required by the Pavement Management Tools. Note that if no data is stored in this table, then the PMS will use internal default data.

The Point Features table (see Figure 17) is a generic table designed to hold point inventory features. These are data that can be described and referenced as a point along a road section. They include items such as Road Signs, Pedestrian Crossings, Junctions and Kilometre Posts (note that Kilometre Posts are described under LRPs in Section ‎‎4.4.6 on Page 9). These are normally picked up as part of a regular road inventory survey or inventory update procedure.
The Inspector is the name given to the survey team that collected the field information on this feature.
The survey_date is the date on which information on this feature was collected.

Roughness Data is stored in the Roughness table as shown in Figure 18. The Roughness table is a historic table. This means that dTIMS allows data to be stored over time on the same road section, so that trends in data can be produced.

Roughness is typically measured using a Bump Integrator or laser equipment, and is measured and stored in dTIMS as IRI (International Roughness Index) in meters / km. Data is normally averaged at 100 meter intervals prior to loading into the RMS. Data are normally collected annually, depending on the policies described in the UNRA Pavement Data Collection Policies and Procedures manual.

Introduction

This Pavement Management System User Manual describes the use of software tools by the Uganda National Roads Authority (UNRA) to generate forward works programs to develop and maintain the national road network. These PMS tools can also be used to predict Asset Value.
The tools covered in this manual are:
dTIMS CT (hereafter referred to as dTIMS) Life Cycle Cost Analyser (LCCA),
Highway Development and Management system (HDM-4) and
Roads Economic Decision Model (RED)
Together these tools form the Pavement Management System (PMS) for UNRA.

dTIMS is the tool on which UNRA’s PMS is stored, maintained and analysed.  UNRA’s road network definition and data is stored in a dTIMS database, also referred to as UNRA’s dTIMS database.  Workflow is an accelerator in dTIMS that automates, standardizes and documents dTIMS processes such as:
importing/exporting of data in UNRA’s dTIMS database,
preparation of  data and information for a life cycle cost analysis,
transformation (manipulation) of data in UNRA’s dTIMS database and
provides access to the data of UNRA’s dTIMS database,
provides access to the database management capabilities in dTIMS, and it
provides access to selected routines and information of any life cycle cost analyses in UNRA’s dTIMS databse.
The Asset Management Policies and Procedures Manual describe the circumstances and frequencies with which these tools should be used.

The Network Planning Unit of the Directorate of Network Planning and Engineering is responsible for using the Pavement Management System to generate forward works programs to develop and maintain the National Road Network. Specifically, the Asset Management Unit is responsible for the activities listed in Figure 1 below.
Setup of default data
Preparation of data in dTIMS prior to export to the Pavement Management System tools
Running HDM-4
Running RED
Presentation of Results in the Geographic Information System (GIS).

Basic models for Vehicle Operating Costs (VOCs), Road Deterioration, and Road Treatment Effects have been established during the customization of this system, based on information and data supplied by UNRA. These models and their parameters may be refined by UNRA in future.

The PMS allows the user to define various default data to be used in cases where actual data is not provided. These might include items such as visual condition data, roughness data, climatic indicators, projected traffic growth rates, etc.
Budget data should be provided to allow generation of forward works programs, optimized under budget constraints.

A series of procedures must be followed to prepare data for the PMS tools. These PMS tools (RED, HDM-4 and dTIMS LCCA) are exact with regards to the format and content of data and input parameters.  After the data has been prepared, then the dTIMS LCCA, HDM-4 and/or RED may be run to generate forward works programs.
The resultant programs can be presented in chart and/or graph form, and may also be presented in UNRA’s Geographical Information System (GIS).

Introduction

The Bridge Management System (BMS) of the Uganda National Roads Authority (UNRA) is an Information Management and Decision Support System.  The BMS makes inventory and inspection data easily accessible and manageable, and inquiries are easily made concerning bridges and major culverts.

The ultimate purpose of the Bridge Management System is to assist officials in ensuring that all bridges and major culverts are acceptably maintained and therefore provide an acceptable and safe level of service to the public.  The expenditure needed to provide this can be determined, and compared to budgetary constraints.  The system incorporates this by providing the following:-
Identification of bridges and major culverts that need preservation activities (preventive maintenance and rehabilitation) and replacement.
Short-term planning and prioritization of all work on bridges and major culverts.
Providing estimates of longer term budgetary requirements to maintain the bridge and major culvert stock at the required level of service.

The level of service can be used as the primary focal point in managing a public facility.  In this case the level of service gives an indication of the degree to which a bridge provides an acceptable and safe facility to the public.  Failure to meet this level of service could result in costs to the user due to the damage to property, or costs associated with delays due to bridge closure.  On the other hand, the authorities that have to maintain these bridges are financed through taxation of the public.  A BMS should therefore minimize the total cost of the public by finding an optimum balance between acceptable levels of service and the cost of providing these levels of service within the budgetary constraints.
The Bridge Management System Database
The database of the BMS is the nucleus of the system.  All the information essential to the management of the bridges and major culverts is contained in the database.  This includes the following:-
Bridge Inventory Data – The data describing each individual bridge.
Bridge Inspection Data – The data that are entered from inspections done on the bridges.
Major Culvert Inventory Data – The data describing each individual culvert.
Major Culvert Inspection Data – The data that are entered from inspections done on culverts.
Photo Module – Includes inventory photos of the bridges or components of the bridges and major culverts, as well as photos of distresses.

A primary objective of the system is to identify those bridges and major culverts that need attention.  In order to do this, bridges are ranked according to a deficiency index.  The index is made up of three basic components, as follows:-
Condition of the Bridge

Maintaining a bridge in an acceptable condition incurs costs.  The lack of maintenance may also lead to user costs, if the condition of the bridge deteriorates to the point where the load capacity of the bridge is affected, or where the wearing surface or joints may damage vehicles.

The relevant importance of each bridge can be measured in terms of the traffic volumes that cross the bridge and the strategic importance of the bridge.  Strategic importance is measured in terms of the potential delays in traveling time if the bridge were to be closed.

The first part of the reporting system is the facility that can be used to print a report on a specific bridge or major culvert using certain criteria.  The “Detection” screen assists the user in finding the number of the bridge or major culvert that a report is required for.  There is a group of standard reports available, once the required structure has been selected.  The following is a list of the standard reports:-
Inventory Report
Condition Report
Inventory Graphs for bridges
Bridges with Principal Inspection Data
Bridges with Superficial Inspection Data
Bridges with Special Inspection Data
Maintenance Plan for a Specific Bridge and Inspection Date
Export of inventory data for bridges based on network detected
Culvert Inventory Graphs
Major Culverts with Principal Inspection Data
Major Culverts with Special Inspection Data
Maintenance Plan for a specific Major Culvert and latest inspection
Export of inventory data for major culverts based on network detected
About this Manual

This manual will assist the user of the Bridge Management System.  The procedures for completing the screens, and for using the various options successfully are discussed in the remaining sections of this document.

NOTE THAT THE BMS REQUIRES THE SHORT DATE FORMAT OF THE COMPUTER WHERE IT IS INSTALLED TO BE ACCORDING TO THE dd/MM/yyyy FORMAT.  (Use the Start/Control Panel/Regional and Language Options/Regional Options/Customize/Date/Short date format to adjust to this format.)

NOTE THAT THE BMS USES THE CURRENCY SETTINGS OF THE COMPUTER WHERE IT IS INSTALLED.  (Use the Start/Control Panel/Regional and Language Options/Regional Options/Customize/Currency/Currency Symbol to adjust to your required setting.)

The system was designed under screen resolution of 800 x 600, but can be viewed under any screen resolution.  At a screen resolution of 800 x 600, use of scroll bars (vertical and horizontal) will be required, at screen resolutions of more than this, it will typically not be required.  Also, at screen resolutions of more than 800 x 600, the screen may appear to be using the top left hand corner of the screen only.  This is normal for these higher resolutions.
General users – these are the users who want to run network reports, data validation reports or view structures according to criteria on a GIS platform.
Access control is via a predefined password for each of the above.
