Introduction

This manual describes the pavement data collection policies and procedures of UNRA.  It confirms UNRA’s commitment to the use of its Pavement Management System to manage its pavements and gravel roads in terms of its corporate vision, mission and goals, as expressed through its Road Infrastructure Asset Management Policy.
Introduction

Pavement Management System (PMS): A set of tools or methods that assist decision makers in finding optimum strategies for providing, evaluating and maintaining pavements in a serviceable condition over a period of time.
Pavement Management: A management approach used by road authority staff to make cost effective decisions regarding providing, evaluating and maintaining pavements, typically by using a PMS.
Roadway register: The list of all roadways under jurisdiction of UNRA.

Shoulder: When the shoulder is referred to as a surface: The area between the outside edge of the carriageway and the shoulder breakpoint. When shoulder is referred to as a pavement layer: The layer on top of the subbase, or, in the absence of a subbase, on top of the subgrade and lying between the outside edge of the base and the shoulder breakpoint.
Use of the PMS to manage the Network of Paved and Unpaved Roadways

The Directorate of Network Planning and Engineering (Network Planning Unit) of UNRA is responsible for the planning of maintenance and repair interventions for the network of paved and unpaved roads (roadways) under UNRA’s jurisdiction.  For this purpose UNRA has procured and implemented a suitable PMS to assist it in the management of the paved and unpaved roads.  It is therefore a policy of UNRA that this network will be managed with its PMS.  This means that:
All proclaimed roads under the jurisdiction of UNRA shall be captured in the RMS and referenced according to the Location Referencing System of UNRA.
The paved and unpaved roadway register will be established in the PMS and kept up to date to reflect all paved and unpaved roadways under UNRA responsibility.
The required related paved and unpaved roadway information of all roadways will be collected and captured in the PMS and will be kept up to date.
The condition of the paved and unpaved roadways will be inspected according to the inspection frequency policy of UNRA, refer Chapter 6.

The paved and unpaved roadway  registers and condition inspections will at all times comply with the data specifications of UNRA (refer UNRA Data Format Specifications for Road Inventory and Condition Inspections (includes inter alia data formats for visual assessment data, machine-based roughness data, video look-up data)).

The paved and unpaved roadway register compilation and condition inspections will be governed by a Quality Management Plan (QMP) to ensure compliance with the specifications.  The QMP will include, as a minimum, quality control and quality verification and acceptance procedures. These will be in response to, and aimed at ensuring compliance with the roadway data specifications of UNRA.

The needs for maintenance and rehabilitation of the roadways under control of UNRA will be determined annually with the PMS. These needs will be based on the most recent condition inspections, and suitably updated unit costs for treatments as defined in the PMS.
UNRA will use the maintenance and repair needs as determined with the PMS to compile its annual and multi-year work plans for roadway maintenance and repair.

UNRA will budget for the operational expenditure of the PMS consisting of the maintenance of the paved and unpaved roadway register, the maintenance of the condition inspection data according to its inspection frequency policies.  Budgeting will include annual software maintenance fees payable to Deighton Associates Limited for the dTIMS software.

Should in-house staff resources and skills diminish to the point that any aspects of the operation of the PMS are at stake, then UNRA will outsource such functions.  UNRA will ensure that all outsourced functions are governed by the same quality control and verification / acceptance procedures than prescribed in this policy document.

UNRA undertakes to provide PMS staff with all necessary skills to execute all required tasks of the data collection for the PMS.  As required, training will be arranged, whether for data collection or operation of the PMS.  In the same regard, staff will be assisted to obtain access to technical literature, and to attend relevant technical conferences, whether presented locally or internationally.
Collecting and updating road network data
Maintaining a road data bank and Road Management System (RMS)
Collecting road and bridge condition data
Formulating short and long-term road and bridge investment plans
Types of Roadway Data Collection
The types of roadway data to be collected consist of inventory data and inspection data.
Roadway inventory data collection consists of:
Initial inventory data collection (newly constructed roadways):

This shall preferably be done from the as-built drawings.  Arrangements shall be made with the relevant directorate to obtain the as-built drawings for extraction of the required network referencing data. The roadway will then be added to the RMS and PMS for the first time.
Initial inventory data collection (previously constructed roadway, but no as-built drawings available):
In this case, the inventory data shall be obtained in the field during a visit to the roadway.  The roadway will then be added to the RMS and PMS for the first time.
Inventory confirmation:

Ideally, all visits to a roadway will be used to confirm the inventory of the roadway (dimensions, types of components) as previously captured in the RMS and PMS. The regular roadway inspections will be used to confirm the roadway inventory as well. The inventory data of roadways already existing in the RMS and PMS will then be corrected, if necessary.
Roadway inspection data collection consists of:
Routine maintenance inspection:

This is a cursory, visual check carried out by road maintenance personnel who have a good practical knowledge of roadway routine maintenance needs, but who are not necessarily specialists in pavement condition inspection.  These inspections are required for the purpose of planning short-term routine road maintenance.  These inspections will normally not provide data for the PMS and are not considered formal enough for updating the PMS.  They do however provide data for the ROMAPS routine maintenance programme.
Pavement visual condition inspection:

This is a regular inspection carried out by a qualified visual condition inspector under the general supervision of a senior pavement engineer, or alternatively by a senior pavement engineer and forms the backbone of an inspection programme required for the PMS.  It will consist of an examination of all the components of the roadway in order to rate the occurrence of a pre-defined series of defects.  Standardised reporting forms and evaluation criteria will be used to aid in the later computerisation of the inspection results.
Pavement equipment surveys:

Pavement equipment surveys shall be carried out using specialized equipment able to measure the profile of the roadways at high speed, typically for roughness. These surveys are regular surveys carried out by qualified operators using certified equipment under the general supervision of a senior pavement engineer, and forms part of the backbone of the regular inspection programme required for the PMS.

Panel inspections are required to confirm the outputs of the needs determination as obtained through the operation of the PMS and shall be executed by a panel of pavement engineers. The panel members shall include the senior pavement engineer who supervised the visual condition inspections and equipment surveys, and the staff involved in the needs determination that lead to the development of the multi-year preventive maintenance and rehabilitation investment plan.

A special inspection will usually be made in connection with unusual circumstances, such as exceptional loading, with the occurrence of a major weakness or with the reassessment of the roadway against revised specifications and regulations.  They will invariably require the detailed involvement of a specialist pavement engineer.  They are also referred to as ‘emergency’ inspections.
Roadway Data Collection Frequencies
The UNRA policy for roadway data collection frequency is listed below.
Data collection item
Data collection frequency
Initial inventory data collection (newly constructed roadways, not yet on the RMS and PMS)
Initial inventory data collection (previously constructed roadway, not yet on the RMS and PMS)
Once it is discovered that a roadway exists that is not on the RMS and PMS.
Inventory confirmation
As soon as possible when doubt has been raised regarding a roadway’s inventory information.
Routine maintenance inspections
Refer to the ROMAPS data collection policies and procedures
Visual condition inspections
However, once a need has been identified for a special inspection, then this is overridden by the special inspection survey frequencies (see below).
Equipment surveys (only for paved roads)
However, once a need has been identified for a special inspection, then this is overridden by the special inspection survey frequencies (see below).
On conclusion of a draft preventive maintenance and rehabilitation needs determination (ie investment plan).
When required by unusual circumstances, eg due to major weakness noticed during visual condition or other inspections.

Despite the roadway data collection frequency policy as defined above for roadway condition inspections, the scale and frequency of roadway inspections are a matter for engineering judgement and must be decided for each situation.  They depend on many factors including:

The classification or importance of the roadway; the sensible use of limited inspection resources demand that a greater priority be given to the visual inspection and equipment surveys on strategically or economically important routes or corridors than to those routes of lower importance or classification.

The age, condition and history of the roadway; evidently, if deterioration has been observed which requires more frequent monitoring, or if a roadway is suspect for a reason, then closer surveillance of the structure would be justified.
The age of specifically the surfacing is important – there is a window of opportunity to apply preventive maintenance, after which rehabilitation is the only suitable treatment option.
Note that a full inspection is required for network performance monitoring.

Appendix AQuality Verification and Acceptance Form for 2017 Paved Road Visual Condition
Surveys
Introduction

A Quality Management Plan (QMP) provides a comprehensive, systematic approach to data collection and processing.  It contains detailed quality verification and acceptance procedures and established guidelines to monitor the entire process.
Quality Control:  The responsibility of the Network Engineer and his staff of visual condition assessors and equipment operators and UNRA management (Network Planning Unit), and
Quality Verification and Acceptance:  The responsibility of UNRA management (via the Quality Auditor) to ensure independent verification and recommendation for acceptance of the collected data.

Quality control includes actions and considerations necessary to assess and adjust production processes to obtain the desired level of quality of pavement condition data.  These activities will then include checks on the equipment used to collect the data, the personnel responsible for the data collection and the data collection process itself.

Quality acceptance activities are those that govern the acceptance of the pavement condition data.  This is often referred to as ‘quality assurance’, but the NCHRP (see footnote) does not use the term quality assurance due to the fact that it is more aimed at all the activities focusing on increasing the ability to fulfil requirements for the product or service being produced.  UNRA thus also does not use the quality assurance term in this QMP.
This Quality Management Plan (QMP) governs the visual assessment data collection, and is aimed at ensuring quality data captured for the PMS.

The over-arching governing principles for pavement data collection are provided in the Pavement Data Collection Policies and Procedures of UNRA.  The QMP is a companion document to the Policies and Procedures document, and shall focus on ensuring quality data.  The latter defines the contents of the QMP as follows:
Personnel training and certification;
Data capture equipment (Mobicap);
Data capture verification;
Data verification procedures;
Data management;
Data management;
Data Collection Cycle
In-house Paved Road Visual Condition Assessment is carried out on the full UNRA paved road network by two (2) teams, comprised of the following members:
Field data collection surveys are to be carried out during months of fourth quarter FY2016-17, to be completed and submitted by end of June 2017.
Data Collection Equipment
Pavement Visual Condition Data Capture Device
Data will be captured using the Mobicap data capture software operating on two (2) tablet devices (one for each team).

The data capture software is specifically configured for the condition assessment approach of UNRA.  Each tablet will be pre-loaded with link information for all Regions.  Teams will also carry hard copies of the information on roads to be surveyed.  All the links are pre-split into one-kilometre visual assessment segments.
Data Collection Quality Control Plan

The purpose of the Quality Control Plan (QCP) is to ensure that the people, processes and equipment are all aligned at collecting quality data.  The QCP thus has to do with the actual data collection exercise, detecting problems as soon as possible, before large quantities of data have to be re-collected.
In terms of the Pavement Data Collection Policies and Procedures of UNRA, the contents of a comprehensive QCP typically include the following elements:
Documented (and available) manuals and procedures;
Training, testing and certification of survey personnel;
Data capture verification and acceptance.
Team Members shall work under direction of the Team Leader in carrying out condition assessment surveys, in compliance of the agreed quality control procedures.

Network Engineer (Quality Control Supervisor) is responsible for the establishment of the quality control component of this QMP, together with the teams.  He/she is also responsible for interim quality verification throughout the data collection cycle.

Network Engineer (Quality Auditor) is responsible for the quality verification and acceptance component of the QMP.  He/she is also responsible for final quality verification of the data collected against the quality acceptance specification.
Personnel Training and Certification
Objective

Personnel operating the data capture tablets and conducting the visual condition assessments shall be properly trained, tested and certified prior to the start of the surveys.  Formal training and testing procedures shall be used in order to improve the consistency and accuracy of the collected data.
Visual Assessment Training
Visual assessment training, testing and certification will consist of theoretical and practical training and testing.

For visual condition assessments a special effort will be required to ensure an acceptable standardized approach.  This is due to the requirement to lower the subjectivity of the defect identification and rating approach.  Of relevance is the manual for visual condition assessments, which will form the basis of the training materials:

Manual for Visual Inspections of Paved Roads must be made available in the survey vehicle (an electronic copy is available on the RMS, in the Visual Condition Assessment user folder, under the file name “UNRA_AssetManagementSystem_2_RoadDataCollection_0.2.pdf”.

The training will deal with the theory of pavement distress and characteristics identification and rating, and practical sessions on paved roads varying in condition from very good to very poor.  Completion of the visual assessments and data capturing will be part of the training.
The training is to be given by the Network Engineer to the survey teams prior to their departure to the field.
Training Evaluation
An evaluation system will be introduced to evaluate skills and shortcomings of staff, in order to identify continuing training needs.
Approach to testing at theoretical training session/s and minimum marks to be achieved:
Multiple choice test, against the data collection manual and against photographs of various road condition distress types and severity levels.  Minimum pass rate is 80%.
Approach to testing at practical training session/s and minimum marks to be achieved:

Visual assessments of 5 to 10km in total of segments, to be compared to assessments of Network Engineer.  Defect by defect comparison should yield less than 20% of defects with differences of 2 or more on the 5-point rating scale.
Test results are to be stored on the RMS Server.
Personnel Certification (for Visual Condition Assessments)
The formal training session consisting of theoretical and practical testing will form part of the personnel certification.
Data Verification Procedures
Visual Assessment Data Verification

The Network Engineer could meet the assessors on the road during their first week of production assessments.  The Network Engineer would also do the assessments on the same roads, and compare the results in their presence.  Should there be any substantial differences, then these shall be discussed and more calibration of the assessors done to achieve acceptable results.

Similar verification during the completion of the production assessments may require considerable effort from the Network Engineer, and prioritization as to which roads are to be verified may be necessary.  However, visual assessment data verification shall not be neglected.

All the results from these verification checks shall be submitted and saved to the RMS Server.  It will include a definite opinion to the acceptance of the data collected to date (by the Network Engineer), and be able to be accessed later during quality acceptance.
Data Capture Software

Direct capturing of data on the road will be done using the Mobicap data capturing software.  The data capture software has built-in controls that will only allow acceptable data, typically from pick lists, or within defined ranges.  Some cross checking of data is also possible, while the software also ensures that all compulsory data is captured.

A hard copy of the user manual for Mobicap should be carried in the survey vehicle. (An electronic copy is available on the RMS, in the Visual Condition Assessment user folder, under the file name “UNRA_AssetManagementSystem_2_RoadDataCollection_0.2.pdf”).
Back-up of captured data onto storage media;
Data and Documentation Management
The Quality Control component includes an agreed approach to data and documentation management.  This includes:
Initial training, testing and verification results;
Backup procedures and backup medium to avoid data loss;

The purpose of the Quality Control Plan (QCP) is to ensure that the people, processes and equipment are all aligned at collecting quality data.  The QCP thus has to do with the actual data collection exercise, detecting problems as soon as possible, before large quantities of data have to be re-collected.
In terms of the Pavement Data Collection Policies and Procedures of UNRA, the contents of a comprehensive QCP typically include the following elements:

He/she is responsible for the quality verification and acceptance component of the QMP.  He/she is also responsible for final quality verification of the data collected.
4.3 Personnel training and certification
4.3.1 Objective

Personnel operating the ROMDAS shall be properly trained, tested and certified prior to the start of any surveys.  Formal training and testing procedures shall be used.   UNRA reiterates that adequate training will improve the consistency and accuracy of the collected data.
4.3.2 Equipment Operator Training
Equipment operator training will consist of theoretical and ‘on-the-job’ training.
All team members shall be familiar with the available training materials.
The following theoretical training session/s were planned and executed on the dates listed:
On-the-job training was executed on the dates listed:
Training materials: These are all user manuals of the various equipment to be used [list only relevant manuals], ie:
[ROMDAS XXXX User Manual (An electronic copy is available on the RMS, in the ROMDAS user manuals folder, under file name: “……………… .pdf”.
Z-250 Class I profiler User Manual (An electronic copy is available on the RMS, in the ROMDAS user manuals folder, under file name: “…………. .pdf”).
Copies of these training materials shall be made available to the UNRA staff.
Equipment operators shall also be familiar with the equipment calibration requirements and checklists for operational procedures (see 4.3 later) and these shall form part of the training materials.
4.3.3 Training Evaluation
An evaluation system will be introduced to evaluate skills and shortcomings of staff, in order to identify continuing training needs.

Currently there are no post commissioning support agreements (Annual Maintenance Subscription, AMS) in place apart from a system diagnostics agreement which is executed over the internet.

In the absence of equipment supplier certification of UNRA or Service Provider (DCC) operators, UNRA will certify the equipment operators through an UNRA Network Engineer (and the Consultant) who presented the training and did the testing.  Such certification will typically only be valid for one contract or one cycle of surveys.
4.4.1 Objective
Verification that the equipment is functioning according to expectations, and that approved methods of operation are followed which are crucial for the data quality control.
A support agreement exists with the ROMDAS equipment supplier for assistance, including an annual system audit to ensure that the system is working properly.

Equipment calibration forms an important part of equipment operation.  Calibration is performed as per the specifications of the ROMDAS supplier, prior to start of surveys, and at 5,000 km intervals or as required.

Equipment supplier recommendations for frequencies of inspections, calibrations etc has been captured onto a series of daily, weekly, monthly, three monthly and annual (as relevant) checklists or logsheets.  Every activity on the logsheet has to be indicated if executed, with comments on findings.  Equipment operators are responsible to date and sign off all logsheets.

Base departure sheets has also been introduced.  These sheets provide a checklist of activities to be done before departing from the base (Kampala), and includes a checklist of equipment, tools, etc to be taken along.  Survey Team Leaders are responsible to date and sign off all base departure sheets.

Verification is the systematic process to validate the data collection equipment by comparing the measurements with a standard reference value (also referred to as control sections or ‘ground truth’) that is considered as correct.  Verification tests are periodic checks before, during and after a cycle of data collection surveys that confirm the continued accuracy of pavement condition data collection by comparing the data with known reference measurements.  The equipment should preferably be verified before, during and after the network measurements.

Profile measurement equipment will be verified against Bump Integrator calibration sections measured with the Z250 profiler to establish the reference profile.  They shall cover the range of typical profiles of the full network, and at least three (3) of the established calibration sections in good, fair and poor condition shall be used.

If the various verification processes identify deficiencies, the equipment will be checked and, if necessary, re-calibrated.  All data (collected since the last satisfactory verification) will be identified and evaluated.  Where found sub-standard, the surveys done since the previous acceptable verification will have to be repeated.   Regular equipment verification serves as an important tool for data collectors to confirm data collection since a previous verification to be acceptable.  However, the establishment of only three control sections possibly close to the base may not provide profile data collection staff sufficient opportunities to do equipment verification, especially if the road network is stretched out far away from these.
4.5 Data Verification Procedures
4.5.1 Equipment Data Verification

During the data collection process a variety of methods is available for ensuring the continued collection of satisfactory quality data.   As part of the Quality Control Plan, UNRA data collection staff shall maximize the use of these to support their claims of acceptable data.

During measurements the collection of the data will be verified in real time in the survey vehicle, to confirm that no gaps in the data collection exist and that production of large quantities of unsatisfactory data is avoided.  The aim is quick detection and correction of errors and minimizing delays and costs associated with poor data quality collection.  Frequent verification of data during production is thus aimed for.

The earlier mentioned periodic re-testing of ‘control sections’ during the profile measurements also provides a data verification opportunity.  Comparison of the equipment measured profiles to the profiles of the control sections will act to confirm that the equipment is still providing acceptable results.    All the results from these verification tests shall be submitted and saved to the RMS Server.  It will include a definite opinion to the acceptance of the data collected to date by the Team Leader of the relevant surveys, and be able to be accessed later during quality acceptance.
Back-up ROMDAS unit / equipment components
Data backup-up storage media
4.7 Data and Documentation Management
The Quality Control component shall include an agreed approach to data and documentation management. This includes:
Results of initial training, testing and certification of equipment operators
Backup procedures and backup medium to avoid data loss
Upload of data to central database

This document is a template for compilation of a Quality Management Plan (QMP) for pavement data collection.  It shall be re-compiled per cycle of data collection or data collection project given the requirements for a QMP under the Pavement Data Collection Policies and Procedures of UNRA.  It confirms UNRA’s commitment to collection of good quality data for use in its Pavement Management System (PMS) to manage its pavements and gravel roads in terms of its corporate vision, mission and goals, as expressed through its Road Infrastructure Asset Management Policy.

The document is compiled under the premise that a QMP is never finalized, and shall be updated as experience (positive and negative) is gained in executing and validating the data collection.  Such updating may even be required to tighten up certain procedures identified to be issues during a previous or a current cycle of data collection, or to introduce additional checking at various stages.

This QMP is based on NCHRP Synthesis 401, Quality Management of Pavement Condition Data Collection.  It was used as base to introduce a best practice QMP for the Network Planning Unit.  The Synthesis summarized current quality management practice of 55 roads authorities in northern America.   The use of the Synthesis in preparation of the QMP is acknowledged with thanks.

Visual and inventory Inspections of Paved and Unpaved Roads
Surface Condition Surveys (ADDENDUM NO. 1)

A visual inspection of the road is conducted to assess the condition of the road surface and immediate areas such as the shoulders and drainage. As a result of this survey the road will be divided into smaller sub-sections where the pavement type and the condition differ. The surveys will be conducted from a slowly moving vehicle driving near to the shoulder of the road enabling the driver of the vehicle to carefully observe the road surface. Frequent stops should be made to enable close examination of the surface and road surroundings. The teams will log the assessments as identified on the day of survey.
During the visual surface condition survey the ratings of the following pavement surface defects are to be recorded:
Drainage Condition;

Each condition parameter is to be collected over a scale of 1 (no defects) to 5 (failed/very poor), apart from potholes which is measured as pothole area in m2. The surface condition will be recorded for the average value determined over every 1000m on both paved and unpaved road sections.

The date of survey will always be recorded and be consistent with the day of survey. If any changes to the road condition have altered from the time of survey, it is not necessary to resurvey and re-qualify the condition.
The resources and the equipment required for the detailed condition survey and the operational details are described in Annex A.
Surveys for Paved Roads
All Cracking is measured as a percentage of the surface area of the rating segment. Five ratings of crack are defined; the precedence that these must be recorded in is as following:
Wide cracking is measured as a percentage of All Cracking.
The loss of load spreading ability and waterproofing will usually lead to accelerated deterioration of the pavement condition. Factors, which lead to cracking, include:
Below are descriptions of the different crack types which during the survey are to be combined into an ’All Cracks’ value which gives a total area of cracks on a given section (area) of the road.

Single cracks are to be included with the area of influence of which they are covered. The area of influence for single cracks is the length of the crack multiplied with the influence area which is set to 30cm of every side of the crack. A single crack on a road section which is 100m long, will therefore have an area of influence of about 60.36m2 [(100+0.3+0.3) x 0.3 x 2] and can be rounded and recorded as 60m2 on the survey form. The area of crocodile cracking is calculated as length multiplied by width in m2 including 30cm on every side.

Potholes are recorded as the number occurring over the 1000m rating segment. Each pothole will be assumed to measure 1m2. Therefore, two small potholes measuring approximately 0.5m2, will be counted as 1 pothole. Similarly, a large pothole measuring approximately 2m2 will be counted as 2 potholes, and so on. Table 1 below provides a guideline to the size of circular potholes.
Width Loss is measured in mm as an average over the whole rating segment. Width Loss is recorded using the following five ratings:
Edge Drop is measured in mm as an average over the whole rating segment. Edge Drop is recorded using the following 5 ratings:

Edge Break occurs along the interface of a bituminous surface pavement and the shoulder is most significantly affected where the shoulder is unsealed. For convenience they are considered to be a defect of the bituminous surfacing and not of the shoulder material. They may occur locally or may be continuous over a length of road. These defects frequently happen on one side of the roadway, or on corners, or where the edge of the pavement is vulnerable to tyre wear and attrition. The detrimental effects of edge defects include:
Reduction of pavement width;
Surface Deterioration is measured as a percentage of surface area of the rating segment. Surface Deterioration is recorded using the following five ratings.

Rutting is load associated deformation and will appear as longitudinal depressions in the wheelpaths. It is the result of an accumulation of non-recoverable vertical strains in the pavement layers and in the subgrade. This type of rutting is not associated with any shoving in the upper layers of the pavement unless it becomes very severe. The rut depth should be measured by excluding the shoving height, and should be the vertical distance between the road surface and the bottom of the rut.
Friction discernible (visible), speed reduction necessary.

As Roadside Friction increases, traffic safety dramatically declines. Areas in which Roadside Friction exist, mainly occurs on linear settlements such as market areas and towns, generally any roadside area with a lot of activity. Severe roadside friction occurs when the obstacles are not limited to the shoulder, but also affect the carriageway. It is important to note that Roadside Friction is time dependent; therefore road surveyors’ must be aware of the time of day the road is surveyed and rate accordingly.
Surveys for Gravel and Earth Roads
The shape and surface condition of unsealed roads change as the surface is continually modified by traffic, environmental agencies (wind, rain, temperature etc.) and maintenance activities.

The presence and significance of deformation of unsealed roads are influenced largely by the frequency and effectiveness of maintenance. Defects will be determined and documented as per the criterion listed in the following.
Severe exposure of sub-grade; Engineered cross section on road non existing on part of the road; Gravel thickness <50mm.
No gravel found on the section 100 percent of sub-grade exposed: Engineered cross section on road non existing

The above scale is for use in visual assessments of the condition of the gravel surface layer. The assessor is expected to confirm this by test holes with intervals of about 10km, with a minimum of one test pits per road. Furthermore, when the assessor detects changes in the Gravel visual characteristics, they are expected to take a physical sample related to location (chainage and GPS coordinates) and road number and return it to the head office for classification and reference purposes.

Potholes in gravel and earth roads are an indication of lack of maintenance usually caused by the gravel wearing course being worn away by vehicles or erosion. Potholes in an unpaved road are bowl shaped depressions in the surface of the road.

Rutting is defined as the maximum deviation measured under a 3 m straight edge placed transversely across the rut. Note that if the rut displays signs of shoving, the distance measured should not include this and should be limited to the distance between where the surface was initially designed to be, and to the bottom of the rut.
Can be felt and heard; No speed reduction necessary.
Can be felt and heard; Speed reduction necessary.
Significant speed reduction necessary.
Drainage Condition
Ride poor and uncomfortable; Frequent unevenness of the profile Significant rutting, corrugations and occasional potholes; Moderate speed reduction; IRI value >=7 and <9.
Inventory Surveys (ADDENDUM NO. 1)

There are two main types of road inventory data to be collected, each of which needs a different and individual data collection treatment. These are ‘Line’ or ‘Continuous’ data and ‘Point’ or ‘Discrete’ data.
All inventory data collection demands significant time and human resource inputs. It can only be done by manually recording the presence and location of each inventory item.
Continuous / Line Data:

This data type relates to data that can be termed continuous and has both a defined ‘Start’ and ‘End’ point to which two chainages and two sets of GPS coordinates can be given and which can be measured therefore in linear or square meters.
It is important to note that there are a number of default Inventory items that MUST be completed for throughout the entire road. These are the following:
Covered;

Road markings will be recorded with a code that identifies the type of road marking. All survey teams will be provided with a copy of Uganda’s official Highway Code, and all road marking types recorded will be consistent with these codes. Road markings can be both line and point features. The following are the line road markings.
Discrete / Point Data
This data type relates to discrete event data that can be adequately described by a single chainage and a single set of coordinates. The following are the point features that are to be collected:

Road Signs will be recorded with a code that identifies the type of road sign. All survey teams will be provided with a copy of Uganda’s official Highway Code, and all road sign types recorded will be consistent with these codes.
The Road sign condition should also be recorded (Good/Fair/Poor).
Data Entry

All teams will be assigned a laptop with a Microsoft Access database pre-installed. Microsoft Access is a relational database management system that combines the relational Microsoft Jet Database Engine with a graphical user interface and software-development tools. The database assigned will allow for data entry through pre-designed Entry Forms, as well as the storage of the data. It is the duty of the teams to populate this database with the results of the survey. This is to be carried out during the inspection and so reducing the possibility that significant information is omitted. Furthermore, it is the duty of the Team Leader to verify the data entered at the end of each road that has been surveyed by completing the verification module provided in the database.

In addition to the database, all teams will be required to complete a “Network Definition” form. This is a paper based form and should be completed for each road surveyed. A schematic diagram should be drawn, as well as the following criteria demonstrated below in Table 2. These paper forms are not for official submission, but form part of the quality control system, and allow for combating any controversial issues that may arise.
Enter a code for the weather during the survey:
The description should follow the established markers for an existing road inventory or a clear description such as junctions or village should be used.
The carriageway width should be measured and, recorded at the beginning of each Road Link.
The shoulder width should be measured and, recorded at the beginning of each Road Link.
The drainage width should be measured and, recorded at the beginning of each Road Link.
Annex ASurface Condition Survey Equipment
The equipment needed by the visual and inventory inspection teams are:
GPS (Handheld for navigation, and an accurate GPS for Inventory);
Laptop with Data Input Forms;

Appendix AQuality Verification and Acceptance Form for 2017 Paved Road Visual Condition
Surveys
Introduction

A Quality Management Plan (QMP) provides a comprehensive, systematic approach to data collection and processing.  It contains detailed quality verification and acceptance procedures and established guidelines to monitor the entire process.
Quality Control:  The responsibility of the Network Engineer and his staff of visual condition assessors and equipment operators and UNRA management (Network Planning Unit), and
Quality Verification and Acceptance:  The responsibility of UNRA management (via the Quality Auditor) to ensure independent verification and recommendation for acceptance of the collected data.

Quality control includes actions and considerations necessary to assess and adjust production processes to obtain the desired level of quality of pavement condition data.  These activities will then include checks on the equipment used to collect the data, the personnel responsible for the data collection and the data collection process itself.

Quality acceptance activities are those that govern the acceptance of the pavement condition data.  This is often referred to as ‘quality assurance’, but the NCHRP (see footnote) does not use the term quality assurance due to the fact that it is more aimed at all the activities focusing on increasing the ability to fulfil requirements for the product or service being produced.  UNRA thus also does not use the quality assurance term in this QMP.
This Quality Management Plan (QMP) governs the visual assessment data collection, and is aimed at ensuring quality data captured for the PMS.

The over-arching governing principles for pavement data collection are provided in the Pavement Data Collection Policies and Procedures of UNRA.  The QMP is a companion document to the Policies and Procedures document, and shall focus on ensuring quality data.  The latter defines the contents of the QMP as follows:
Personnel training and certification;
Data capture equipment (Mobicap);
Data capture verification;
Data verification procedures;
Data management;
Data management;
Data Collection Cycle
In-house Paved Road Visual Condition Assessment is carried out on the full UNRA paved road network by two (2) teams, comprised of the following members:
Field data collection surveys are to be carried out during months of fourth quarter FY2016-17, to be completed and submitted by end of June 2017.
Data Collection Equipment
Pavement Visual Condition Data Capture Device
Data will be captured using the Mobicap data capture software operating on two (2) tablet devices (one for each team).

The data capture software is specifically configured for the condition assessment approach of UNRA.  Each tablet will be pre-loaded with link information for all Regions.  Teams will also carry hard copies of the information on roads to be surveyed.  All the links are pre-split into one-kilometre visual assessment segments.
Data Collection Quality Control Plan

The purpose of the Quality Control Plan (QCP) is to ensure that the people, processes and equipment are all aligned at collecting quality data.  The QCP thus has to do with the actual data collection exercise, detecting problems as soon as possible, before large quantities of data have to be re-collected.
In terms of the Pavement Data Collection Policies and Procedures of UNRA, the contents of a comprehensive QCP typically include the following elements:
Documented (and available) manuals and procedures;
Training, testing and certification of survey personnel;
Data capture verification and acceptance.
Team Members shall work under direction of the Team Leader in carrying out condition assessment surveys, in compliance of the agreed quality control procedures.

Network Engineer (Quality Control Supervisor) is responsible for the establishment of the quality control component of this QMP, together with the teams.  He/she is also responsible for interim quality verification throughout the data collection cycle.

Network Engineer (Quality Auditor) is responsible for the quality verification and acceptance component of the QMP.  He/she is also responsible for final quality verification of the data collected against the quality acceptance specification.
Personnel Training and Certification
Objective

Personnel operating the data capture tablets and conducting the visual condition assessments shall be properly trained, tested and certified prior to the start of the surveys.  Formal training and testing procedures shall be used in order to improve the consistency and accuracy of the collected data.
Visual Assessment Training
Visual assessment training, testing and certification will consist of theoretical and practical training and testing.

For visual condition assessments a special effort will be required to ensure an acceptable standardized approach.  This is due to the requirement to lower the subjectivity of the defect identification and rating approach.  Of relevance is the manual for visual condition assessments, which will form the basis of the training materials:

Manual for Visual Inspections of Paved Roads must be made available in the survey vehicle (an electronic copy is available on the RMS, in the Visual Condition Assessment user folder, under the file name “UNRA_AssetManagementSystem_2_RoadDataCollection_0.2.pdf”.

The training will deal with the theory of pavement distress and characteristics identification and rating, and practical sessions on paved roads varying in condition from very good to very poor.  Completion of the visual assessments and data capturing will be part of the training.
The training is to be given by the Network Engineer to the survey teams prior to their departure to the field.
Training Evaluation
An evaluation system will be introduced to evaluate skills and shortcomings of staff, in order to identify continuing training needs.
Approach to testing at theoretical training session/s and minimum marks to be achieved:
Multiple choice test, against the data collection manual and against photographs of various road condition distress types and severity levels.  Minimum pass rate is 80%.
Approach to testing at practical training session/s and minimum marks to be achieved:

Visual assessments of 5 to 10km in total of segments, to be compared to assessments of Network Engineer.  Defect by defect comparison should yield less than 20% of defects with differences of 2 or more on the 5-point rating scale.
Test results are to be stored on the RMS Server.
Personnel Certification (for Visual Condition Assessments)
The formal training session consisting of theoretical and practical testing will form part of the personnel certification.
Data Verification Procedures
Visual Assessment Data Verification

The Network Engineer could meet the assessors on the road during their first week of production assessments.  The Network Engineer would also do the assessments on the same roads, and compare the results in their presence.  Should there be any substantial differences, then these shall be discussed and more calibration of the assessors done to achieve acceptable results.

Similar verification during the completion of the production assessments may require considerable effort from the Network Engineer, and prioritization as to which roads are to be verified may be necessary.  However, visual assessment data verification shall not be neglected.

All the results from these verification checks shall be submitted and saved to the RMS Server.  It will include a definite opinion to the acceptance of the data collected to date (by the Network Engineer), and be able to be accessed later during quality acceptance.
Data Capture Software

Direct capturing of data on the road will be done using the Mobicap data capturing software.  The data capture software has built-in controls that will only allow acceptable data, typically from pick lists, or within defined ranges.  Some cross checking of data is also possible, while the software also ensures that all compulsory data is captured.

A hard copy of the user manual for Mobicap should be carried in the survey vehicle. (An electronic copy is available on the RMS, in the Visual Condition Assessment user folder, under the file name “UNRA_AssetManagementSystem_2_RoadDataCollection_0.2.pdf”).
Back-up of captured data onto storage media;
Data and Documentation Management
The Quality Control component includes an agreed approach to data and documentation management.  This includes:
Initial training, testing and verification results;
Backup procedures and backup medium to avoid data loss;

Personnel operating the ROMDAS shall be properly trained, tested and certified prior to the start of any surveys.  Formal training and testing procedures shall be used.   UNRA reiterates that adequate training will improve the consistency and accuracy of the collected data.

Equipment operator training will consist of theoretical and ‘on-the-job’ training.
All team members shall be familiar with the available training materials.

Currently there are no post commissioning support agreements (Annual Maintenance Subscription, AMS) in place apart from a system diagnostics agreement which is executed over the internet.

In the absence of equipment supplier certification of UNRA or Service Provider (DCC) operators, UNRA will certify the equipment operators through an UNRA Network Engineer (and the Consultant) who presented the training and did the testing.  Such certification will typically only be valid for one contract or one cycle of surveys.
4.4.1 Objective
Verification that the equipment is functioning according to expectations, and that approved methods of operation are followed which are crucial for the data quality control.
A support agreement exists with the ROMDAS equipment supplier for assistance, including an annual system audit to ensure that the system is working properly.

Equipment calibration forms an important part of equipment operation.  Calibration is performed as per the specifications of the ROMDAS supplier, prior to start of surveys, and at 5,000 km intervals or as required.

Equipment supplier recommendations for frequencies of inspections, calibrations etc has been captured onto a series of daily, weekly, monthly, three monthly and annual (as relevant) checklists or logsheets.  Every activity on the logsheet has to be indicated if executed, with comments on findings.  Equipment operators are responsible to date and sign off all logsheets.

Base departure sheets has also been introduced.  These sheets provide a checklist of activities to be done before departing from the base (Kampala), and includes a checklist of equipment, tools, etc to be taken along.  Survey Team Leaders are responsible to date and sign off all base departure sheets.

Verification is the systematic process to validate the data collection equipment by comparing the measurements with a standard reference value (also referred to as control sections or ‘ground truth’) that is considered as correct.  Verification tests are periodic checks before, during and after a cycle of data collection surveys that confirm the continued accuracy of pavement condition data collection by comparing the data with known reference measurements.  The equipment should preferably be verified before, during and after the network measurements.

Profile measurement equipment will be verified against Bump Integrator calibration sections measured with the Z250 profiler to establish the reference profile.  They shall cover the range of typical profiles of the full network, and at least three (3) of the established calibration sections in good, fair and poor condition shall be used.

If the various verification processes identify deficiencies, the equipment will be checked and, if necessary, re-calibrated.  All data (collected since the last satisfactory verification) will be identified and evaluated.  Where found sub-standard, the surveys done since the previous acceptable verification will have to be repeated.   Regular equipment verification serves as an important tool for data collectors to confirm data collection since a previous verification to be acceptable.  However, the establishment of only three control sections possibly close to the base may not provide profile data collection staff sufficient opportunities to do equipment verification, especially if the road network is stretched out far away from these.
4.5 Data Verification Procedures
4.5.1 Equipment Data Verification

During the data collection process a variety of methods is available for ensuring the continued collection of satisfactory quality data.   As part of the Quality Control Plan, UNRA data collection staff shall maximize the use of these to support their claims of acceptable data.

During measurements the collection of the data will be verified in real time in the survey vehicle, to confirm that no gaps in the data collection exist and that production of large quantities of unsatisfactory data is avoided.  The aim is quick detection and correction of errors and minimizing delays and costs associated with poor data quality collection.  Frequent verification of data during production is thus aimed for.

The earlier mentioned periodic re-testing of ‘control sections’ during the profile measurements also provides a data verification opportunity.  Comparison of the equipment measured profiles to the profiles of the control sections will act to confirm that the equipment is still providing acceptable results.    All the results from these verification tests shall be submitted and saved to the RMS Server.  It will include a definite opinion to the acceptance of the data collected to date by the Team Leader of the relevant surveys, and be able to be accessed later during quality acceptance.
Back-up ROMDAS unit / equipment components
Data backup-up storage media
4.7 Data and Documentation Management
The Quality Control component shall include an agreed approach to data and documentation management. This includes:
Results of initial training, testing and certification of equipment operators
Backup procedures and backup medium to avoid data loss
Upload of data to central database
DATA COLLECTION QUALITY VERIFICATION AND ACCEPTANCE PLAN

keyword_paragraphs: Quality Management Plan Template for Pavement Data Collection
Name of specific data collection cycle
1.INTRODUCTION1
2.THIS DATA COLLECTION CONTRACT / CYCLE2
3.DATA COLLECTION EQUIPMENT3
4.DATA COLLECTION QUALITY CONTROL PLAN3
5.DATA COLLECTION QUALITY VERIFICATION AND ACCEPTANCE PLAN10
INTRODUCTION
[Aspects to be considered during the compilation of the QMP are provided in italics between square brackets “[ ]”.  They shall be carefully considered for the specific data collection purpose.

This document is a template for compilation of a Quality Management Plan (QMP) for pavement data collection.  It shall be re-compiled per cycle of data collection or data collection project given the requirements for a QMP under the Pavement Data Collection Policies and Procedures of UNRA.  It confirms UNRA’s commitment to collection of good quality data for use in its Pavement Management System (PMS) to manage its pavements and gravel roads in terms of its corporate vision, mission and goals, as expressed through its Road Infrastructure Asset Management Policy.

The document is compiled under the premise that a QMP is never finalized, and shall be updated as experience (positive and negative) is gained in executing and validating the data collection.  Such updating may even be required to tighten up certain procedures identified to be issues during a previous or a current cycle of data collection, or to introduce additional checking at various stages.

This QMP is based on NCHRP Synthesis 401, Quality Management of Pavement Condition Data Collection.  It was used as base to introduce a best practice QMP for the Network Planning Unit.  The Synthesis summarized current quality management practice of 55 roads authorities in northern America.   The use of the Synthesis in preparation of the QMP is acknowledged with thanks.

A QMP provides a comprehensive, systematic approach to data collection and processing.  It contains detailed quality verification and acceptance procedures and established guidelines to monitor the entire process].

Quality Control component: The responsibility of the Pavement Engineer and his staff of visual condition assessors and equipment operators (whether in-house or out-sourced) and UNRA management (the Network Planning Unit), and

Quality Verification and Acceptance component: The responsibility of UNRA management (via the Quality Auditor or Roadway Data Collection Contract Manager), often delegated to a third party, to ensure independent verification and recommendation for acceptance of the collected data.
[Should the data collection be done in-house, then the data collection team(s) and supervisors will be responsible for compiling both components of the QMP.

Should the data collection be out-sourced, then the data collection service provider(s) will be requested in the tender document to compile a draft quality control component, responding to the data specification.  UNRA (Quality Auditor and Roadway Data Collection Contract manager) will review the submitted Quality Control Component, and finalise this with the service provider prior to the start of the data collection contract. The tender (and contract document) will already contain UNRA’s proposed Quality Verification and Acceptance component.]

‘Quality control includes actions and considerations necessary to assess and adjust production processes to obtain the desired level of quality of pavement condition data’ (refer NCHRP 401).  These activities will then include checks on the equipment used to collect the data, the personnel responsible for the data collection and the data collection process itself.

‘Quality acceptance activities are those that govern the acceptance of the pavement condition data’ (refer NCHRP 401).  This is often referred to as ‘quality assurance’, but the NCHRP does not use the term quality assurance due to the fact that it is more aimed at all the activities focusing on increasing the ability to fulfill requirements for the product or service being produced.  UNRA thus also does not use the quality assurance term in this QMP.
This Quality Management Plan (QMP) governs the data collection, and is aimed at ensuring quality data captured in the PMS.

The over-arching governing principles for pavement data collection are provided in the Pavement Data Collection Policies and Procedures of UNRA. The QMP is a companion document to the Policies and Procedures document, and shall focus on ensuring quality data.  The latter defines the contents of the QMP as follows:
Personnel training and certification
Equipment and method calibration, certification, inspection and verification (if equipment will be used during the data collection)
Data capture verification
Data verification procedures
Software for data checks
Data management
Data management

Where more than one DCC is simultaneously involved in a data collection contract, separate QMPs shall be prepared together with each DCC.
Where UNRA collects data in-house a QMP shall be prepared together with each team.]

DATA COLLECTION QUALITY CONTROL PLAN

[IMPORTANT NOTE: It is a specific requirement of all data collection, whether done in-house by UNRA staff or on contract by DCC staff, that the data collection staff and their supervisors significantly contribute to the establishment of the Quality Control component of this QMP.

The successful application of this Quality Control component depends as much on UNRA as employer as on the data collection staff.  The Quality Control component provides a win-win opportunity for both parties:  For UNRA, it means that acceptable data is collected, for the DCC or UNRA’s own data collection staff it means that opportunities are created to ensure that risks in data collection (and thus the need for re-collection) are mitigated as far as possible.  Therefore, the Quality Verification and Acceptance component of the QMP, should not later generate any surprises.

The purpose of the Quality Control Plan (QCP) is to ensure that the people, processes and equipment are all aligned at collecting quality data.  The QCP thus has to do with the actual data collection exercise, detecting problems as soon as possible, before large quantities of data have to be re-collected.
In terms of the Pavement Data Collection Policies and Procedures of UNRA, the contents of a comprehensive QCP typically include the following elements:
Documented (and available) manuals and procedures;
Training, testing and certification of survey personnel;
Equipment and / or process quality verification procedures before starting and during production testing;
Checks for data reasonableness, consistency and completeness; and
Data capture verification and acceptance.
4.2 Personnel training and certification
4.2.1 Objective

Personnel operating the PMS equipment or conducting the visual condition assessments, shall be properly trained, tested and certified prior to the start of any surveys.  Formal training and testing procedures shall be used.   UNRA and the DCC reiterate that adequate training will improve the consistency and accuracy of the collected data.
4.2.2 Equipment Operator Training
Equipment operator training will consist of theoretical and ‘on-the-job’ training.
All staff ([DCC or UNRA] pavement engineer, all equipment operators) shall be familiar with the available training materials.
The following theoretical training session/s were planned and executed on the dates listed:
On-the-job training was executed on the dates listed:
Training materials: These are all user manuals of the various equipment to be used [list only relevant manuals], ie:
[ROMDAS XXXX User Manual (An electronic copy is available on the RMS, in the ROMDAS user manuals folder, under file name: “……………… .pdf”.
Z-250 Class I profiler User Manual (An electronic copy is available on the RMS, in the ROMDAS user manuals folder, under file name: “…………. .pdf”).
LWFD User Manual (An electronic copy is available on the RMS, in the LWFD user manual folder, under file name: “………………. .pdf”).
GPR User Manual (An electronic copy is available on the RMS, in the GPR user manual folder, under file name: “………………. .pdf”).
Copies of these training materials shall be made available to [the DCC (or UNRA staff for in-house data collection)].
Equipment operators shall also be familiar with the equipment calibration requirements and checklists for operational procedures (see 4.3 later) and these shall form part of the training materials.
Training Evaluation
An evaluation system will be introduced to evaluate skills and shortcomings of staff, in order to identify continuing training needs.

The only way currently to get official certification for personnel operating equipment is through involvement of the equipment suppliers, specifically for the operation of the equipment.  Possibly the only way to achieve this is through visits of the suppliers’ trainers to UNRA.  [Currently there are no post commissioning support agreements (Annual Maintenance Subscription, AMS) in place (confirm the status quo……………), and the Network Management Unit will actively market such agreements with UNRA Management.  Annual visits by suppliers to check the equipment (refer 4.3) can be effectively used for training, testing and certification of staff as well (assess contents of the AMS if it includes visits to UNRA).]

[In the absence of equipment supplier certification of UNRA or Service Provider (DCC) operators, UNRA will certify the equipment operators through an UNRA pavement engineer who presented the training and did the testing.  Such certification will typically only be valid for one contract or one cycle of surveys.]
4.2.3 Visual Assessment Training
Visual assessment training, testing and certification will consist of theoretical and practical training and testing.

For visual condition assessments a special effort will be required to ensure an acceptable standardized approach.  This is due to the requirement to lower the subjectivity of the defect identification and rating approach.  Of relevance is the manual for visual condition assessments, which will form the basis of the training materials:
Manual for Visual Inspections of Paved and Unpaved Roads (An electronic copy is available on the RMS, in the Visual Condition Assessment user folder, under the file name: [“…..    .docx”]).

The training will deal with the theory of pavement distress and characteristics identification and rating, and practical sessions on paved and unpaved roads varying in condition from very good to very poor.  Completion of the visual assessment forms, and subsequent data capturing in the relevant data capture programs will be part of the training.  [Direct capturing on tablets is preferred]
Training Evaluation
An evaluation system will be introduced to evaluate skills and shortcomings of staff, in order to identify continuing training needs.

Inventory Compilation of Paved and Unpaved Roads

This document contains the Manual for the Visual Condition Assessment and Inventory Compilation of Paved and Unpaved Roads of the Uganda National Roads Authority (UNRA). It forms part of the Asset Management System documentation as shown on the previous page.
MANUAL FOR VISUAL CONDITION ASSESSMENT and Inventory COMPILATION of Paved and Unpaved Roads
1Visual Condition Assessment Surveys1
1.1.9Shoulder Condition32
1.2.8Drainage Condition (Drainage off the road)48
2Inventory Surveys52
2.1Continuous / Line Data:52
2.2Discrete / Point Data57
3Data Entry61
Annex ASurface Condition Survey Equipment63
Visual CONDITION ASSESSMENT AND INVENTORY COMPILATION of Paved and Unpaved Roads
Visual Condition Assessment Surveys

A visual inspection of each road link is conducted to assess the condition of the road surface and immediate areas such as the shoulders and drainage. For the purpose of this survey the road link will be divided into shorter sub-sections, termed visual assessment segments, of 1km length. The surveys will be conducted from a slowly moving vehicle driving near to the shoulder of the road enabling the driver of the vehicle (visual condition assessor) to carefully observe the road surface. Frequent stops should be made to enable close examination of the surface and road surroundings. During at least two of these stops the assessor should exit the vehicle. The teams will log the visual assessments as identified on the day of survey.
During the visual condition survey the ratings of the following pavement defects and other characteristics are to be recorded:
Shoulder Condition;

Each condition parameter is to be collected over a rating scale of 1 (no defects) to 5 (failed/very poor), apart from potholes which is measured as pothole area in m2. The condition will be recorded for the most predominate occurrence of the value determined over every 1km visual assessment segment for both paved and unpaved road segments.  The descriptions of the rating scales will assist in this, and it is not necessarily the worst noted values that should be recorded.  Recording the worst noted values as representative of the visual assessment segment may show the segment in worst condition than it actually is. For rutting, it can also be indicated if the recorded values are of localised / isolated occurrence (isolated problem).

The date of survey will always be recorded and be consistent with the day of survey. If any changes to the road condition have altered from the time of survey, it is not necessary to resurvey and re-qualify the condition.
The resources and the equipment required for the detailed condition survey and the operational details are described in Annex A.

The visual assessment surveys shall only be executed once the visual assessors have complied with the agreed pre-survey quality control requirements. Overall, the survey shall comply with the Quality Control Component of the Quality Management Plan (QMP), and be subjected to the post-survey Quality Verification and Acceptance Component as well. For every survey, a QMP shall be prepared.  More details are provided in the QMP template.

‘All Cracking’ is measured as a percentage of the surface area of the rateable visual assessment segment. Five ratings of crack are defined; the precedence that these must be recorded in is as following:
The loss of load spreading ability and waterproofing will usually lead to accelerated deterioration of the pavement condition.

Below are descriptions of the different crack types which during the survey are to be combined into an ’All Cracks’ value which gives a total area of cracks on a given visual assessment segment (area) of the road.

The ‘Wide Cracks’ measure only refers to structural fatigue cracking, as would mostly be occurring in the wheel paths. Any line cracks more than 3mm wide in the wheel paths qualify as Wide Cracks. Crocodile cracking is also predominant in the wheel paths, and often do not reach such (3mm) crack widths. However, where secondary defects have manifested, such as spalling and deformation, they should also be considered as Wide Cracks.

Single cracks are to be included with the area of influence of which they are covered. The area of influence for single cracks is the length of the crack multiplied with the influence area which is set to 30cm of every side of the crack. A single crack of 100m length, will therefore have an area of influence of about 60.4m2 [(100+0.3+0.3) x 0.3 x 2] and can be rounded and recorded as 60m2 on the survey form. The area of crocodile cracking is calculated as length multiplied by width in m2 including 30cm on every side.
Shrinkage of the bituminous surfacing as a result of reduced binder volume, due to the binder ageing and losing its lighter oils and aromatics.

Potholes and Failures are recorded as the number occurring over the 1km visual assessment segment. Each pothole and failure counted will be assumed to measure 1m2. Therefore, two small potholes or failures measuring approximately 0.5m2, will be counted as 1 pothole. Similarly, a large pothole or failure measuring approximately 2m2 will be counted as 2 potholes, and so on. Table 1 below provides a guideline to the m2 size of circular potholes / failures for known diameters.
Caused by loss of shear strength in the base course as a result of deterioration of the layer materials or by excessive moisture in the layer. Traffic induced.
Edge Drop is measured in mm as an average over the whole visual segment. Edge Drop is recorded using the following 5 ratings:

Edge Break occurs along the interface of a bituminous surface pavement and the shoulder is most significantly affected where the shoulder is unsealed. For convenience they are considered to be a defect of the bituminous surfacing and not of the shoulder material. They may occur locally or may be continuous over a length of road. These defects frequently happen on one side of the roadway, or on corners, or where the edge of the pavement is vulnerable to tyre wear and attrition. The detrimental effects of edge defects include:
Reduction of pavement width;

Surface Deterioration is defined as ravelling of surface dressings and disintegration of asphalt surfacings.  They are measured as a percentage of the surface area of the visual assessment segment. Surface Deterioration is recorded using the following five ratings.

Bleeding occurs when excess binder moves upwards relative to the aggregate and then reduces the surface texture depth. Obviously, rating of bleeding will be complicated by the difference in texture obtained on the different forms of surfacing, for example surface dressing versus asphalt. Average bleeding is recorded using the following five ratings.
Occurs when excess binder moves upwards relative to the aggregates and reduces the surface texture depth.
Problems to be considered are:
Presence of high shoulders, edge built-up, poor shoulder maintenance, preventing water run-off;

Drainage off the road refers to drainage by side drains, mitre drains and cross drainage underneath the road through culverts. Although problems with drainage off the road is not a pavement distress type, and thus not an indication of pavement condition, such problems could lead to premature pavement failure. Drainage off the road is rated separately for the left and right sides of the visual assessment segment.

Drainage off the road is recorded in each rateable visual assessment segment, as a number derived from the following five point scale, for each of the Left and Right Drainage off the Road condition separately:
Shoulder Condition
Shoulder condition is rated in terms of the availability of the shoulder as a safe recovery area for vehicles. The Left and Right Shoulder Conditions are rated separately.

For the purpose of the assessment, a shoulder is defined as a 2m width strip from the edge of the pavement (in case of no paved shoulder) or the yellow line (in case of a paved shoulder).  If the paved shoulder is less than 2m wide, the verge (unpaved area) next to the paved shoulder has to be rated as well. Altogether a 2m width has to be considered.
Poor condition of the paved shoulder
Shoulder is unsafe to use as stopping area at the posted speed limit on the relevant side of the segment. Substantial work is required to render the shoulder safe.
Shoulder condition
Condition of the shoulder, including edge drop, shoulder material, erosion, shoulder width and cross section, vegetation growth.
Poor condition due to neglected maintenance
Friction discernible (visible), speed reduction necessary.

As Roadside Friction increases, traffic safety dramatically declines. Areas in which Roadside Friction exist, mainly occur on linear settlements such as market areas and towns, generally any roadside area with a lot of activity. Severe roadside friction occurs when the obstacles are not limited to the shoulder, but also affect the carriageway. It is important to note that Roadside Friction is time dependent; therefore road surveyors’ must be aware of the time of day the road is surveyed and rate accordingly.
The shape and surface condition of unsealed roads change as the surface is continually modified by traffic, environmental agencies (wind, rain, temperature etc.) and maintenance activities.

The presence and significance of deformation of unsealed roads are influenced largely by the frequency and effectiveness of maintenance. Defects will be determined and documented as per the criterion listed in the following.
Severe exposure of sub-grade; or Engineered cross section on road non existing on part of the segment; Gravel thickness <50mm.
No gravel found on the segment; 100 percent of sub-grade exposed; Engineered cross section on road non existing.

If so required by a specific scope of work of data collection consultants, or for a specific survey, the assessor shall confirm the gravel thickness ratings by test holes with intervals of about 10km, with a minimum of one representative test pit per link. The measured thickness should be representative of the layer thickness over the interval. For earth roads, the thickness is recorded as 0mm. Furthermore, if so required, when the assessor detects changes in the gravel visual characteristics, he / she shall take a physical sample related to location (chainage and GPS coordinates) and link number and return it to the head office for classification and reference purposes.
Material Quality refers to the material on which road users are travelling at the time of the visual assessment survey.
The material should have a range of particle sizes ranging from very fine up to about 40mm, which will then provide a strong framework of stones interlocked by a tight matrix of fines.
An excessive amount of large stones shall be avoided – these will result in poor riding quality and difficulty with maintenance.
Roughness of the road is the major performance parameter affecting driver and passenger comfort and safety.
Ride poor and uncomfortable; Frequent unevenness of the profile Significant rutting, corrugations and occasional potholes; Moderate speed reduction; IRI value >=7 and <9.

Potholes in gravel and earth roads are an indication of lack of maintenance usually caused by the gravel wearing course being worn away by vehicles or erosion. Potholes in an unpaved road are bowl shaped depressions in the surface of the road.

Rutting is defined as the maximum deviation measured under a 2m straight edge placed transversely across the rut. Note that if the rut displays signs of shoving, the rutting estimated should not include this and should be limited to the distance between where the surface was initially designed to be, and to the bottom of the rut.
Can be felt and heard; no speed reduction necessary.
Can be felt and heard; speed reduction necessary.
Significant speed reduction necessary.
Newly constructed engineered gravel roads typically have a good formation level, but can loose this due to neglect.
Drainage Condition (Drainage off the road)

As for Paved Roads, Drainage off the Road refers to drainage by side drains, mitre drains and cross drainage underneath the road through culverts. Although problems with drainage off the road is not an unpaved road distress type, and thus not an indication of unpaved road condition, such problems could lead to premature unpaved road failure. Drainage off the road is rated separately for the left and right sides of the visual assessment segment.

Drainage off the road is recorded in each rateable visual assessment segment, as a number derived from the following five point scale, for each of the Left and Right Drainage off the Road condition separately:
Also indicate if any of the following is only a severe isolated problem on the segment (therefore, as rated, it is not predominant on the segment length):
Inventory Surveys

There are two main types of road inventory data to be collected, each of which needs a different and individual data collection treatment. These are ‘Line’ or ‘Continuous’ data and ‘Point’ or ‘Discrete’ data.
All inventory data collection demands significant time and human resource inputs. It can only be done by manually recording the presence and location of each inventory item.
Continuous / Line Data:

This data type relates to data that can be termed continuous and has both a defined ‘Start’ and ‘End’ point to which two chainages and two sets of GPS coordinates can be given and which can be measured therefore in linear or square meters.
It is important to note that there are a number of default Inventory items that MUST be completed for throughout the entire road. These are the following:
Covered;

Road markings will be recorded with a code that identifies the type of road marking. All survey teams will be provided with a copy of Uganda’s official Highway Code, and all road marking types recorded will be consistent with these codes. Road markings can be both line and point features. The following are the line road markings.
Discrete / Point Data
This data type relates to discrete event data that can be adequately described by a single chainage and a single set of coordinates. The following are the point features that are to be collected:

Road Signs will be recorded with a code that identifies the type of road sign. All survey teams will be provided with a copy of Uganda’s official Highway Code, and all road sign types recorded will be consistent with these codes.
