Introduction

The Bridge Management System (BMS) of the Uganda National Roads Authority (UNRA) is an Information Management and Decision Support System.  The BMS makes inventory and inspection data easily accessible and manageable, and inquiries are easily made concerning bridges and major culverts.

The ultimate purpose of the Bridge Management System is to assist officials in ensuring that all bridges and major culverts are acceptably maintained and therefore provide an acceptable and safe level of service to the public.  The expenditure needed to provide this can be determined, and compared to budgetary constraints.  The system incorporates this by providing the following:-
Identification of bridges and major culverts that need preservation activities (preventive maintenance and rehabilitation) and replacement.
Short-term planning and prioritization of all work on bridges and major culverts.
Providing estimates of longer term budgetary requirements to maintain the bridge and major culvert stock at the required level of service.

The level of service can be used as the primary focal point in managing a public facility.  In this case the level of service gives an indication of the degree to which a bridge provides an acceptable and safe facility to the public.  Failure to meet this level of service could result in costs to the user due to the damage to property, or costs associated with delays due to bridge closure.  On the other hand, the authorities that have to maintain these bridges are financed through taxation of the public.  A BMS should therefore minimize the total cost of the public by finding an optimum balance between acceptable levels of service and the cost of providing these levels of service within the budgetary constraints.
The Bridge Management System Database
The database of the BMS is the nucleus of the system.  All the information essential to the management of the bridges and major culverts is contained in the database.  This includes the following:-
Bridge Inventory Data – The data describing each individual bridge.
Bridge Inspection Data – The data that are entered from inspections done on the bridges.
Major Culvert Inventory Data – The data describing each individual culvert.
Major Culvert Inspection Data – The data that are entered from inspections done on culverts.
Photo Module – Includes inventory photos of the bridges or components of the bridges and major culverts, as well as photos of distresses.

A primary objective of the system is to identify those bridges and major culverts that need attention.  In order to do this, bridges are ranked according to a deficiency index.  The index is made up of three basic components, as follows:-
Condition of the Bridge

Maintaining a bridge in an acceptable condition incurs costs.  The lack of maintenance may also lead to user costs, if the condition of the bridge deteriorates to the point where the load capacity of the bridge is affected, or where the wearing surface or joints may damage vehicles.

The relevant importance of each bridge can be measured in terms of the traffic volumes that cross the bridge and the strategic importance of the bridge.  Strategic importance is measured in terms of the potential delays in traveling time if the bridge were to be closed.

The first part of the reporting system is the facility that can be used to print a report on a specific bridge or major culvert using certain criteria.  The “Detection” screen assists the user in finding the number of the bridge or major culvert that a report is required for.  There is a group of standard reports available, once the required structure has been selected.  The following is a list of the standard reports:-
Inventory Report
Condition Report
Inventory Graphs for bridges
Bridges with Principal Inspection Data
Bridges with Superficial Inspection Data
Bridges with Special Inspection Data
Maintenance Plan for a Specific Bridge and Inspection Date
Export of inventory data for bridges based on network detected
Culvert Inventory Graphs
Major Culverts with Principal Inspection Data
Major Culverts with Special Inspection Data
Maintenance Plan for a specific Major Culvert and latest inspection
Export of inventory data for major culverts based on network detected
About this Manual

This manual will assist the user of the Bridge Management System.  The procedures for completing the screens, and for using the various options successfully are discussed in the remaining sections of this document.

NOTE THAT THE BMS REQUIRES THE SHORT DATE FORMAT OF THE COMPUTER WHERE IT IS INSTALLED TO BE ACCORDING TO THE dd/MM/yyyy FORMAT.  (Use the Start/Control Panel/Regional and Language Options/Regional Options/Customize/Date/Short date format to adjust to this format.)

NOTE THAT THE BMS USES THE CURRENCY SETTINGS OF THE COMPUTER WHERE IT IS INSTALLED.  (Use the Start/Control Panel/Regional and Language Options/Regional Options/Customize/Currency/Currency Symbol to adjust to your required setting.)

The system was designed under screen resolution of 800 x 600, but can be viewed under any screen resolution.  At a screen resolution of 800 x 600, use of scroll bars (vertical and horizontal) will be required, at screen resolutions of more than this, it will typically not be required.  Also, at screen resolutions of more than 800 x 600, the screen may appear to be using the top left hand corner of the screen only.  This is normal for these higher resolutions.
General users – these are the users who want to run network reports, data validation reports or view structures according to criteria on a GIS platform.
Access control is via a predefined password for each of the above.

INTRODUCTION

This manual is written for the GIS-Viewer Administrator.  It covers preparing and installing the GIS-Viewer client application on a suitable workstation or device. The GIS-Viewer administrator should be familiar with the GIS-Viewer desktop application before reading this manual.

The GIS-Viewer installation media referenced in this manual is the media containing all the relevant files and folders required to install the application. This can mean a CD, USB memory card, USB external hard drive or a Network folder. It is assumed that the administrator has access to these files and has a basic knowledge of what is where.
GIS-Viewer INSTALLATION procedure

In the GIS_Install folder, double click on the GISSetup_Server.exe or GISSetup_Client.exe file – depending whether the installation is done on the client or the server.  Note that the installation on the server is required before the installation on the client can be done successfully. Install each of the options starting at the top and follow the instructions described under the sections below. Moving the mouse to the correct will highlight the row, then click on the button.

NB. If this is already installed this step can be skipped.  This step ensure that all the necessary pre-requisite files are installed for the GIS Viewer to run without errors on both the server as well as the client.
Server Only: Install Postgres Database V9.3
Click on: Install Postgres Database V9.3
NB. If the database is already installed this step can be skipped.
By default it is c:\program files (x86)\postgresql\9.3 for Windows 64 bit
and c:\program files (x86)\postgresql\9.3 for Windows 32 bit.
Also leave the default directory for the data files
We suggest to keep the password postgres but it can be changed - just remember the password when starting the GIS_Viewer application for the first time.
Keep the default port 5432 unless you have another Postgres database using this port.
Server Only: Install Postgres Database Add-Ins
Click on: Install Postgres Database Add-Ins
NB. If the database add-ins is already installed this step can be skipped.
NB. Make sure to mark the tick Create spatial database and click Next
Leave the default destination folder (This should be the same as the installation directory as the Postgres Database selected above)
Leave the User Name as postgres and enter the password and port chosen in the Postgres database installation. (Default is postgres and 5432)
This will just create a sample database - leave as is - click install
Server and Client: Install GIS-Viewer Vx.x.x Application Files
Click on: Install BMS-GIS-Viewer Vx.x.x Application Files

Open Windows Explorer and open the C:\Databases\BMS\GIS folder - search in the right pane for the BMS_GIS_Start.exe file. Sometimes the '.exe' will be hidden - just find the BMS_GIS_Start application file.
The shortcut is now created. To rename the shortcut, click on it and press F2, now type the text you want e.g. BMS-GIS
NB. If the database connection is already established this step can be skipped.
The installation process will install the ODBC driver required by the GIS_Viewer application and Reports.
Preparing the GIS Database on the server
Opening the GIS Database (Postgres)
NB. This step is required after installation.
If it does not exist here look for it under All Programs
The database will be opened

Double click on the server: PostgreSQL 9.3 (localhost:5432). The password will be asked when the database is opened for the first time. Enter your password (default is postgres) and mark the tick Store Password - click OK.
Create a new postgres Database
The screen will open showing you all databases if you click on Databases
Right click on Database and click New Database
At name enter bms and select postgres at owner. Leave the rest and click OK.
The newly created database is now shown in the object browser
NB. You don’t need to restore a database as everything will be created when you run the BMS-GIS-Viewer for the first time.
Go to: C:\Program Files\PostgreSQL\9.3\data
Or: C:\Program Files (x86)\PostgreSQL\9.3\data (for 64 bit Windows)
Restart Postgres (Ensure any tomcat instances pointed to the databases on the server are stopped.)
The pg_hba.conf file is like an ACL (Access Control List) for the database. It only allows connections from servers that match the ACL list.
The file can restrict access based on IP, username and database and any combination of the above.
An example entry that will allow all IP's and all usernames to connect to all databases is below.
psql: FATAL: no pg_hba.conf entry for host "192.168.15.56", user "postgres", database "openclinica", SSL off

The above message is saying your IP is "192.168.15.66", your user is "postgres" and you are trying to connect to database "openclinica." Based on this information, your new pg_hba.conf configuration line would be the following:
Backup/Restore the GIS Database on the server
Restore a Postgres Database.
Right click on the new database (bms) and click restore.
Click the ... button to select the database filename located on the install CD/Disk. Select the file with a date e.g. 20160315.backup
You now have restored the database for bms.
Backup a Postgres Database
Right click on the new database (bms) and click backup.
Click the ... button to enter a database filename to store the database on the Disk. Enter a file with a date e.g. 20160315.backup
You now have a backup of the database for bms.

INTRODUCTION
This manual is written for the BMS-GIS-Viewer User.  The GIS-Viewer application must be installed properly as described in the latest installation manual e.g. Installation Manual V1.2.docx

NB. If you want to run the application for the first time, Right click on the shortcut and select Run as Administrator and wait for the GIS-Viewer application to open. (If you run the application and no error is displayed, the database setup is done properly and you don’t need to run as Administrator again)

If any one of the following error/s are displayed, you may need to run the application as Administrator (as described above) and/or you should run the GIS-Viewer Setup and make sure the application is started as Administrator:
If the following error is displayed, click No and do the Maps Setup again as described in section: Update Shapefiles under GIS-Viewer Setup
GIS-Viewer Setup
Start the GIS-Viewer and then Click on Setup
Mark this to specify that this PC is the Server PC. The BMS-Access database and the GIS database is running on this PC.
This is the mapped drive to the server PC. If you open Windows explorer in this Drive you should see the BMS Access Database and the GIS folder in the drive.
BMS Access Database:
This is the name of the BMS Access Database. NB. Not the path but only the name.
BMS Main Window:
This is the beginning of the Window Name of the BMS Access Application. Used to switch between the BMS and GIS applications.
If the photos are stored in a different path, the full path where the photos reside, should be entered here.
Maximize GIS Application:
When checked, the GIS-Viewer will be maximized when started.
When checked all points with zero coordinates (Longitude AND Latitude) will be ignored when searching for points.
PostgresSQL Database:

This is the folder where the Postgres database application is installed. This is only applicable on the Server. You will see this message if you are trying to save and Postgres is not installed on your PC. Just click Yes to ignore this error. You have to copy all shapefiles from the server to the client PC’s when it is updated.
For Windows 32 bit - c:\program files\postgresql\9.3\bin\
For Windows 64 bit - c:\program files (x86)\postgresql\9.3\bin\
This is the Hostname of your GIS Database server – on the Server it will default back to Localhost. (You can find this name on the Server PC under PC Name)
This is the Name of your computer. This is useful to know the Server name that needs to be entered at Hostname on the Client PC’s.
Database Name:
This is the Name of your Postgres Database on the server. Ask the Administrator to help you with this information.
Database User:
This is the User of your Postgres Database on the server. Ask the Administrator to help you with this information.
Database Password:
This is the Password of your Postgres Database on the server. Ask the Administrator to help you with this information.
This is the ODBC name for the connection to the Postgres database. Leave as BMS.
When Points are selected and displayed on the map and you click on the map for information on the point, the closest point within this parameter will be searched for.
Click the Save button to save the information. Checks will be done if the information is correctly entered.
Update Point Data

NB. This can be done on any PC. This is required when running for the first time to load all Point Data Information. This must also be done before Creating Shapefiles for the first time. Do this also every time new Points are loaded into the BMS Access database to update the GIS information.
Start the GIS-Viewer and then Click on Setup
All the latest point information will be saved in the GIS Database. This can be executed as often as new point information is loaded.

NB. The shapefiles will be created on the Server PC. This is required when running for the first time to create all shapefiles. If an error occurs, make sure you update the Point Data first and try again.
Start the GIS-Viewer and then Click on Setup

NB. The shapefiles will be created on the Server PC. This is required when running for the first time to create all shapefiles. If an error occurs, make sure you update the Point Data first and try again.
BMS-GIS Viewer application
Double click on the Shortcut and wait for the GIS-Viewer application to open.

All Layers marked with are visible. Click in the box to toggle visibility. When the application is closed, the last visibility settings are stored and when the application is opened the saved visibility options is restored
Basic information about the PC and the BMS-GIS-Viewer is displayed.
Switch back to the main BMS Application
Click on the Switch to main App button and if the application is open it will be displayed in the foreground. If not check you setup that the BMS Main Window Name is entered and spelled correctly.
Click on the Load Points button and the following screen will be displayed. All previously loaded points will be cleared when new points are loaded.
There is a frame around and the text is in a bigger font.
Click on the Load Points button and the following screen will be displayed. All previously selected/loaded points will be cleared when new points are loaded.
- select this option to measure a distance on the map. Click on the map once and then again and again etc. Double click to end and the measure line will be displayed in Red.

- This status bar at the bottom of the map will always displays the Longitude/Latitude location of the mouse cursor on the map. Sometimes more information is displayed to the right e.g. distance measured

Introduction

The Bridge Management System (BMS) of the Uganda National Roads Authority (UNRA) is a Management Information and Decision Support System.  The BMS makes inventory and inspection data easily accessible and manageable, and inquiries are easily made concerning bridges.
Identification of bridges that need maintenance, rehabilitation and replacement.
Planning and prioritization of all work on bridges.
Providing estimates of longer term budgetary requirements to maintain the bridge system at the required level of service.

The level of service can be used as the primary focal point in managing a public facility.  In this case the level of service gives an indication of the degree to which a bridge provides an acceptable and safe facility to the public.  Failure to meet this level of service could result in costs to the user due to the damage to property, or costs associated with delays due to bridge closure.  On the other hand, the authorities that have to maintain these bridges are financed through taxation of the public.  A BMS should therefore minimize the total cost of the public by finding an optimum balance between acceptable levels of service and the cost of providing these levels of service within the budgetary constraints.
The Bridge Management System Database
The database of the BMS is the nucleus of the system.  All the information essential to the management of the bridges is contained in the database.  This includes the following:-
Bridge Inventory Data – The data describing each individual bridge.
Inspection Data – The data that are entered from inspection done on the bridges.
Maintenance Needs – The complete maintenance as well as the outstanding maintenance item required for each bridge.
About the program

A primary objective of the system is to identify those bridges that need attention.  In order to do this, bridges are ranked according to a deficiency index.  The index is made up of three basic components, as follows:-
Condition of the Bridge

Maintaining a bridge in an acceptable condition incurs costs.  The lack of maintenance may also lead to user costs, if the condition of the bridge deteriorates to the point where the load capacity of the bridge is affected, or where joints may damage vehicles.

The relevant importance of each bridge can be measured in terms of the traffic volumes that cross the bridge and the strategic importance of the bridge.  Strategic importance is measured in terms of the potential delays in traveling time if the bridge were to be closed.
Maintenance and Short Term Budget Needs Module

Bridge inspectors indicate the type, quantity, deterioration and urgency of work required on a bridge by means of standard reporting forms.  The system then optimizes the selection of work within a specified budgetary constraint.  This is achieved by giving preference to firstly, the most urgent and secondly, the most cost-effective requirements.  The remaining budgets and estimated costs of outstanding items are calculated in detail.

The first part of the reporting system is the facility that you can use to print a report on a specific bridge using certain criteria.  The “Detection” screen assists you in finding the number of the bridge that you want a report on.  There is a group of standard reports available, that you use, once you have selected the required structure.  The following is a list of the standard reports:-
Inventory Report.
Inspection Report with Maintenance Recommendations.
Short Term Budget Prediction
Bridges with Periodic Inspection Data
Bridges with Routine Inspection Data
About this Manual
This manual will assist the user of the Bridge Management System.  The procedures for completing the screens successfully are discussed in the remaining sections of this document.

NOTE THAT THE BMS REQUIRES THE SHORT DATE FORMAT OF THE COMPUTER WHERE IT IS INSTALLED TO BE ACCORDING TO THE dd/MM/yyyy FORMAT.  (Use the Start/Control Panel/Regional and Language Options/Regional Options/Customize/Date/Short date format to adjust to this format.)

NOTE THAT THE BMS USES THE CURRENCY SETTINGS OF THE COMPUTER WHERE IT IS INSTALLED.  (Use the Start/Control Panel/Regional and Language Options/Regional Options/Customize/Currency/Currency Symbol to adjust to your required setting.)

Appendix A – dTIMS Design and Setup for the PMS
Introduction

This Pavement Management System User Manual describes the use of software tools by the Uganda National Roads Authority (UNRA) to generate forward works programs to develop and maintain the national road network. These PMS tools can also be used to predict Asset Value.
The tools covered in this manual are:
the dTIMSTM CT (hereafter referred to as dTIMS) Life Cycle Cost Analyser (LCCA)
the Highway Development and Management system (HDM-4)
the Roads Economic Decision Model (RED)
Together these tools form the Pavement Management System (PMS) for UNRA.
The Asset Management System Policies and Procedures Manual describe the circumstances and frequencies with which these tools should be used.

The Asset Management Unit of the Directorate of Planning is responsible for using the Pavement Management System to generate forward works programs to develop and maintain the National Road Network. Specifically, the Asset Management Unit is responsible for the activities listed in Figure 1 below.
Setup of default data
Preparation of data in dTIMS prior to export to the Pavement Management System tools
Running HDM-4
Running RED
Presentation of Results in the Geographic Information System (GIS).

Basic models for Vehicle Operating Costs (VOCs), Road Deterioration, and Road Treatment Effects have been established during the customization of this system, based on information and data supplied by UNRA. These models and their parameters may be refined by UNRA in future.

The PMS allows the user to define various default data to be used in cases where actual data is not provided. These might include items such as visual condition data, roughness data, climatic indicators,  projected traffic growth rates, etc.
Budget data should be provided to allow generation of forward works programs, optimized under budget constraints.

A series of procedures must be followed to prepare data for the PMS tools. These PMS tools (RED, HDM-4 and dTIMS LCCA) are exact with regards to the format and content of data and input parameters.  After the data has been prepared, then the dTIMS LCCA, HDM-4 and/or RED may be run to generate forward works programs.
The resultant programs can be presented in chart and/or graph form, and may also be presented in UNRA’s Geographical Information System (GIS).
Figure 2 - PMS System Overview
RED system
Introduction
RED is the Roads Economic Decision Model for Economic Evaluation of Low Volume Roads.   According to the Africa Technical Transport Note:
HDM-4 is not particularly customized for low-volume roads (less than 200 vehicles per day),
HDM-4 does not capture all the benefits associated with rural investments and
HDM-4 requires a series of inputs which are impractical to collect for low traffic levels.

RED is presented as a simplified economic evaluation model to fulfill the planning and programming need of highway agencies in charge of low-volume roads, without demanding input parameters that may be unrealistic and costly to collect while presenting the results in a practical and effective manner.

The data exported from the dTIMS database include all roads and should thus be filtered by the PMS user to include selected low-volume roads only.  It will be up to the user to decide which low-volume road segments should be analysed; the software makes provision for 285 segments to be analyzed at a time.

The RED software is a set of Excel files with spread sheets programmed with macros and formulas for the purpose of analysing maintenance and/or rehabilitation alternatives.  Whenever reference is made to RED or the RED software, it implicates the Excel files.  The main files that form the RED software are a VOC file, a MAIN file, a PROGRAM file and a RISK file.  An additional Excel file (DATA FROM dTIMS)  was added to customise the road network data that was exported from dTIMS to be used in the RED program file.

All files customised for use by UNRA, reside with documentation and related files  on the directory C:\UNRA_PMS\RED.  Along with these files, the executable file EBM-32.EXE  should reside on the directory C:\UNRA_PMS\RED, otherwise the budget optimisation feature of the RED software will not work.  There are also backups files under C:\UNRA_PMS\RED\UNRA Masterfiles in case the working files become corrupted or invalid.  Never change the information in the backup directory and files, but copy them to the working directory; C:\UNRA_PMS\RED  before proceeding to the preparation of data stage or analysis stage.
UNRA RED - HDM-4 VOC models.xls – file containing the parameters necessary to develop VOC models.

These Draft Pavement and Bridge Maintenance Management Strategies were produced by Roughton International Limited in Joint Venture with Aurecon AMEI Limited for Uganda National Roads Authority for the project, Asset Management Support to UNRA.
Document Ref: PAVEMENT AND BRIDGE MAINTENANCE MANAGEMENT STRATEGIES
Report: Draft Pavement and Bridge Maintenance Management Strategies
This report is submitted as a deliverable of the Asset Management Support project to UNRA.
DRAFT PAVEMENT AND BRIDGE MAINTENANCE MANAGEMENT STRATEGIES

Two draft maintenance management strategies, one for pavement maintenance management and one for structures (bridges and major culverts) maintenance management,  have been developed under the consultancy support services for road infrastructure asset management.  These strategies are in support of the Draft Road Infrastructure Asset Management Policy (RIAMP) and the Draft Strategic Asset Management Plan (SAMP) of UNRA.  The strategies shall guide the development of short and medium-term work plans and Investment Plans for the pavement and structure assets of UNRA.

Maintenance management establishment component, which includes the management arrangements for asset maintenance across UNRA, and consideration of service delivery objectives and status of the relevant assets;

Technical component, which focusses on the important aspect of planning of technical activities, outlining the planning and decision making to be undertaken, including each of the categories of maintenance work that must be incorporated, in an appropriate balance, into the maintenance strategy;
Procurement component, being the strategy to be developed for procurement of maintenance services for a portfolio of maintenance projects;
Maintenance design / execution component, concerning the design and the physical execution of the maintenance interventions, whether executed in-house or through contractors; and

Maintenance review component, which completes the maintenance management cycle of activities through a review process, aimed at reviewing the effectiveness of the maintenance management strategy regarding the above-mentioned components.

Not all of the recommended activities of the strategy components can be implemented from the start, due to current lack of required data, tools and competence and it is recommended that all activities be scheduled for implementation over a number of years.   It shall also further be noted that any strategy is a work-in-progress, representing Top Management’s guidance to achieve its corporate and asset management objectives.   Continuous updating and refinement of the strategies is thus necessary.
This document includes two draft maintenance management strategies in appendices, one for each of pavement maintenance and structures maintenance.

SECTION ONE: INTRODUCTIONSECTION ONE: INTRODUCTION
SECTION ONE: INTRODUCTION
SECTION ONE: INTRODUCTION
INTRODUCTION
Objectives of the Project
The main objectives of the consultancy support services are to ensure that:
The data in the UNRA RMS is accurate, complete and up to date;
UNRA is able to prepare its short, medium and long-term road and bridge maintenance plans and management reports;
UNRA’s operations are based on a Road Asset Management Plan;
General Scope of the Services
In order to achieve the objectives above, the Consultant is performing the tasks and services supporting UNRA to:
Quality Assure all data to be imported into the RMS and to ensure that the data imported into the System is accurate and complete;
Prepare UNRA’s annual prioritised work plans by performing Life Cycle Cost Analysis (LCCA) on the entire national road network;
Scope of this Document

This document provides background to the development of draft pavement and bridge maintenance management strategies and was prepared in fulfilment of the requirements of Chapter 5.2 (Pavement Management Support Services – Finalise Pavement Maintenance Management Strategy) and Chapter 5.3 (Bridge Management Support Services – Finalise Bridge Maintenance Management Strategy).

It is important to note that maintenance management strategy development is not a once-of activity. A strategy is management’s response to guide activities in order to achieve or continue to achieve its corporate objectives.  Should anything change that will affect the corporate objectives, and thus the asset management objectives, then the strategy will have to be reviewed to ensure that the objectives can still be achieved. Therefore, the maintenance management strategies for pavements and bridges presented in this document can at best be a ‘template’ for future updating by UNRA.  Furthermore, the strategies are also future focussed, with some objectives achievable now, but some only achievable over the long-term.

The Draft Pavement and Bridge Maintenance Management Strategies will require internal discussion, evaluation, workshops and pilot usage for number of years, while technical procedures and details re being documented, under guidance of Top Management. Scheduled implementation with continuous review and updating of the strategies should follow thereafter.
SECTION TWO: DRAFT PAVEMENT AND BRIDGE MAINTENANCE MANAGEMENT STRATEGIES DEVELOPMENTSECTION TWO: DRAFT PAVEMENT AND BRIDGE MAINTENANCE MANAGEMENT STRATEGIES DEVELOPMENT
SECTION TWO: DRAFT PAVEMENT AND BRIDGE MAINTENANCE MANAGEMENT STRATEGIES DEVELOPMENT
SECTION TWO: DRAFT PAVEMENT AND BRIDGE MAINTENANCE MANAGEMENT STRATEGIES DEVELOPMENT
DRAFT PAVEMENT AND BRIDGE MAINTENANCE MANAGEMENT STRATEGIES development

This section introduces the development of the Draft Pavement and Bridge Maintenance Management Strategies and their relationship to the Draft Road Infrastructure Asset Management Policy (RIAMP), as well as the general SAMP.  Some background to the development of the Pavement and Bridge Maintenance Management Strategies is provided, followed by a discussion of the proposed contents of the strategy documents.

This task is aimed at developing Draft Pavement and Bridge Maintenance Management Strategies.  At this time, UNRA has been provided with the following, which are under consideration for adoption and implementation:
Draft Data Collection Policies and Procedures, v1.1, March 2016; and
Draft Quality Management Plan Templates for pavement, bridge, traffic and GPS data collection, v1.0, March 2016.

UNRA specifically requested in the ToR that further strategies be developed for pavement and bridge maintenance management.  Chapter 5.2 (Pavement Management Support Services – Finalise Pavement Maintenance Management Strategy) and Chapter 5.3 (Bridge Management Support Services – Finalise Bridge Maintenance Management Strategy) state as follows:

“A proposed pavement maintenance management strategy is currently included with the general road asset management policy / strategy. There is a need to prepare a separate more focussed pavement maintenance and improvement management strategy which shall detail the standards with treatment triggers with costs. Support shall be provided to prepare a pavement maintenance management strategy.”
“The Consultant shall prepare a focussed bridge maintenance management strategy.”

The reference to the ‘general road asset management policy / strategy’ under the pavement maintenance management strategy is considered to be a reference to the 2012 document with the title “Asset Management Policies and Procedures”.
provides a framework for setting asset management objectives;
Planning to achieve asset management objectives;

The figure shows that at strategic level, the Asset Management Policy (RIAMP) takes account of the organisational plans and objectives, as well as other policies, and guides the development of the asset management strategy.   “Other strategies” referred to at the bottom of the figure includes inter alia the Pavement and Bridge Maintenance Management Strategies which are the subject of this document.  As such, they are also relevant at strategic level.

At operational level, asset portfolio plans and asset specific plans are developed taking account of strategic direction as embodied in the asset management policy and asset management strategy, and other strategies such as the “Pavement and Bridge Maintenance Management Strategies”.

This document was issued in 2012 as the Draft “Asset Management Policies and Procedures”.  It is comprehensive and provides a “road infrastructure maintenance strategy framework”.  The road infrastructure maintenance strategy consists of a combination of the ends (goals) for which UNRA is striving and the means (objectives) by which it is seeking to get there. The strategy is thus the road map of the path chosen to move towards the end vision. The Road Infrastructure Maintenance Strategy is developed around the six components of Planning, Budgeting, Scheduling, Design / Execution, Feedback / Reporting and Monitoring / Evaluation.
Integrated road infrastructure planning and programming
Planning road infrastructure maintenance
According to sound and benchmarked principles for road infrastructure maintenance management
Based on current, relevant and sound data and information
Considering life cycle needs for all maintenance types (routine, preventive, rehabilitation, upgrading, repairs), also abandonment and downgrading
Including public opinion surveys
Objectives (means) listed to achieve the goals for Planning are defined as:
Prioritising and differentiating
Data needs for planning
Execution options for maintenance
Training for planning
Public opinion surveys
Budgeting for road infrastructure maintenance
According to a top-down budgeting approach within a cooperative framework among Head Office, Regional and Station staff
Identify and quantify backlogs and recommended proposed funding levels to reduce the backlog over some time
Motivate and justify budget needs in terms of benefits of maintenance
Objectives (means) listed to achieve the goals for Budgeting are defined as:
Training in the budgeting process
Scheduling of road infrastructure maintenance
Four goals for Scheduling are set as follows – Maintenance will be scheduled:
At Head Office level to schedule preventive maintenance, rehabilitation, upgrading and major bridge repairs
At Station level to prepare monthly, weekly and daily work schedules in terms of goals set forth in the annual work plan and prioritisation criteria

Aurecon South Africa (Pty) Ltd configured the RED software for exclusive use of UNRA.

The World Bank (the copyright owner of the HDM-4 software) grants the license holder of the enclosed software (Uganda National Roads Authority), the right to install the enclosed application at any number of computer locations for exclusive use within the licensee’s organization in accordance with the license agreement between UNRA and the World Bank as dictated by the software download agreement.
The license holder may make copies of the application, for archival purposes (for the purpose of backing-up the software and protecting the investment from loss).

This software, and the printed manuals enclosed, whether prepared by the copyright owner or Aurecon, may be used by any number of people and may be freely moved from one computer to another, as long as the users are part of the licensee’s organisation. Appendix A – dTIMS Design and Setup for the PMS
Introduction

This Pavement Management System User Manual describes the use of software tools by the Uganda National Roads Authority (UNRA) to generate forward works programs to develop and maintain the national road network. These PMS tools can also be used to predict Asset Value.
The tools covered in this manual are:
the dTIMSTM CT (hereafter referred to as dTIMS) Life Cycle Cost Analyser (LCCA)
the Highway Development and Management system (HDM-4)
the Roads Economic Decision Model (RED)
Together these tools form the Pavement Management System (PMS) for UNRA.
The Asset Management System Policies and Procedures Manual describe the circumstances and frequencies with which these tools should be used.

The Asset Management Unit of the Directorate of Planning is responsible for using the Pavement Management System to generate forward works programs to develop and maintain the National Road Network. Specifically, the Asset Management Unit is responsible for the activities listed in Figure 1 below.
Setup of default data
Preparation of data in dTIMS prior to export to the Pavement Management System tools
Running HDM-4
Running RED
Presentation of Results in the Geographic Information System (GIS).

Basic models for Vehicle Operating Costs (VOCs), Road Deterioration, and Road Treatment Effects have been established during the customization of this system, based on information and data supplied by UNRA. These models and their parameters may be refined by UNRA in future.

The PMS allows the user to define various default data to be used in cases where actual data is not provided. These might include items such as visual condition data, roughness data, climatic indicators,  projected traffic growth rates, etc.
Budget data should be provided to allow generation of forward works programs, optimized under budget constraints.

A series of procedures must be followed to prepare data for the PMS tools. These PMS tools (RED, HDM-4 and dTIMS LCCA) are exact with regards to the format and content of data and input parameters.  After the data has been prepared, then the dTIMS LCCA, HDM-4 and/or RED may be run to generate forward works programs.
The resultant programs can be presented in chart and/or graph form, and may also be presented in UNRA’s Geographical Information System (GIS).
Figure 2 - PMS System Overview
RED system
Introduction
RED is the Roads Economic Decision Model for Economic Evaluation of Low Volume Roads.   According to the Africa Technical Transport Note:
HDM-4 is not particularly customized for low-volume roads (less than 200 vehicles per day),
HDM-4 does not capture all the benefits associated with rural investments and
HDM-4 requires a series of inputs which are impractical to collect for low traffic levels.

RED is presented as a simplified economic evaluation model to fulfill the planning and programming need of highway agencies in charge of low-volume roads, without demanding input parameters that may be unrealistic and costly to collect while presenting the results in a practical and effective manner.

The data exported from the dTIMS database include all roads and should thus be filtered by the PMS user to include selected low-volume roads only.  It will be up to the user to decide which low-volume road segments should be analysed; the software makes provision for 285 segments to be analyzed at a time.

The RED software is a set of Excel files with spread sheets programmed with macros and formulas for the purpose of analysing maintenance and/or rehabilitation alternatives.  Whenever reference is made to RED or the RED software, it implicates the Excel files.  The main files that form the RED software are a VOC file, a MAIN file, a PROGRAM file and a RISK file.  An additional Excel file (DATA FROM dTIMS)  was added to customise the road network data that was exported from dTIMS to be used in the RED program file.

All files customised for use by UNRA, reside with documentation and related files  on the directory C:\UNRA_PMS\RED.  Along with these files, the executable file EBM-32.EXE  should reside on the directory C:\UNRA_PMS\RED, otherwise the budget optimisation feature of the RED software will not work.  There are also backups files under C:\UNRA_PMS\RED\UNRA Masterfiles in case the working files become corrupted or invalid.  Never change the information in the backup directory and files, but copy them to the working directory; C:\UNRA_PMS\RED  before proceeding to the preparation of data stage or analysis stage.
UNRA RED - HDM-4 VOC models.xls – file containing the parameters necessary to develop VOC models.
